The Pattern You Found Effective
1. Structure: "PM Feedback → Design Feedback" Split When you asked for design critiques, I gave you two distinct sections:

PM feedback first — focused on product thinking, user psychology, the "why" behind design choices
Design feedback — specific visual/technical problems
This separation let you understand both what's wrong and why it matters, not just "make this blue instead of red."

2. The "Here's What's Wrong" → "Here's the Fix" Format I didn't just say "this needs work" — I gave you:

Critical Issues (numbered, specific)
What's happening (the current broken code/approach)
Fix (exact code or approach to use)
Why this matters (the principle behind it)
3. Complete Cursor Prompts When you needed to build something, I gave you comprehensive prompts formatted like:

## CONTEXT
(what file, what component, current state)

## PROBLEMS TO FIX
(specific issues numbered)

## REDESIGN SPECIFICATIONS
(section by section, with code examples)

## FINAL CHECKLIST
(things to verify after implementation)
4. Real Examples + Principles I referenced real products (Stripe, Linear, Notion) not just for inspiration but to show you what professional looks like — then extracted the principles (rhythm, hierarchy, contrast) so you understood why those examples work.

5. Direct + Blunt When Needed When something was wrong, I said it plainly: "This looks like a wireframe, not a production app" or "The emojis scream prototype." No hedging.

Design Feedback

Generic auth UI — looks like every SaaS login ever (centered card, email/password fields, blue button)
Competing messaging — "Sign in to Dokimos" vs. "For Businesses" vs. demo credentials all fighting for attention
No brand consistency — doesn't match the emotional weight of your user-side screens (which have that Instrument Serif headline moment)

Preserves the two-sided login split (user vs. business)

No email field, no password, no form validation. Just context + one CTA.

Tradeoff: Still burns 10 seconds of demo time, but gives you a narrative beat to transition from "user side" to "business side."

Cursor says this but I keep on getting ascreen that's just loading. 

Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.
### Cause
The session bootstrap useEffect depended on **[publicView, router]**. With the Next.js App Router, the object returned by **useRouter() can change identity on every render**. That made the effect run again and again, which repeatedly called setSession / setAuthReady / fetchRequests and felt like the page was **constantly reloading**.
### Fix
router was **removed from the dependency array** so the effect only depends on **publicView**. It still uses router.replace() / router.push() in callbacks; those don’t need to be in the deps for this pattern. A short comment in the file explains why.
### What you should do
1. Restart the dev server (Ctrl+C, then npm run dev in dokimos-app-v2).
2. Open **http://localhost:8081/business** again (hard refresh if needed: Ctrl+Shift+R).
You should get a stable dashboard with **Viewing as: Airbnb** and no reload loop. If anything still flickers, say what you see in the browser console (Network + any errors).

Show more
2:09 PM
The Problem (Diagnosis)
What Cursor fixed:

useEffect dependency array had [publicView, router]
Next.js App Router causes router object to change identity on every render
This triggered infinite re-renders → loading loop
The fix:

Removed router from dependency array
Now only depends on publicView
Why You're Still Stuck on Loading
Even though Cursor fixed the infinite loop, you're still seeing a loading screen. Here's what's likely happening:

Problem 1: Dev server cache Next.js can cache the broken state. Restarting (Ctrl+C → npm run dev) sometimes isn't enough.

Problem 2: Browser cache Your browser cached the old broken page. Ctrl+Shift+R might not clear everything.

Problem 3: The loading state never resolves Something in the session/data fetching logic is still hanging — the loop is fixed, but now it's just stuck waiting.

Then open http://localhost:8081/business in an incognito window (this bypasses all browser cache).

Open DevTools (F12), go to Console tab. Look for:

Red errors (JavaScript exceptions)
Warnings about failed requests
Any messages about auth/session/fetching
Paste what you see here. Even if it looks like gibberish, I need to see it.

Step 3: Check Network Tab
In DevTools, go to Network tab. Refresh the page. Look for:

Any requests stuck in "Pending" state
Failed requests (red status codes)
Requests to /api/... that never complete
What requests do you see? Are any stuck or failing?

Open dokimos-app-v2/app/business/page.tsx and find the loading logic. It probably looks like:

tsx

  return <div>Loading...</div>; // or similar
}
Temporarily bypass this to see if data is actually loading:

2:10 PM
PM Feedback First
The infinite loop is fixed, but now you're stuck in loading state. The useEffect dependency fix stopped the constant re-rendering, but the actual data/auth initialization logic is still broken. The page is waiting for something that never arrives.

Design Feedback (Root Cause)
Looking at the code flow Cursor described:

The problem: The session bootstrap logic depends on async operations (setSession, setAuthReady, fetchRequests) that need to complete before authReady becomes true. But something in that chain is hanging.

Why this happens:

authReady state stays false
Component renders <div>Loading...</div>
Nothing triggers authReady to become true
You're stuck forever
Common causes:

fetchRequests() is calling an API endpoint that doesn't exist or is timing out
Session initialization never completes
The conditional logic for authReady has the wrong condition
The Fix (Complete Cursor Prompt)
The business dashboard is stuck on "Loading..." forever. The useEffect dependency fix stopped the infinite loop, but now authReady never becomes true.

CONTEXT:
- File: dokimos-app-v2/app/business/page.tsx
- Current state: Shows "Loading..." and never progresses to dashboard
- Problem: Session/auth initialization is hanging

ROOT CAUSE:
The session bootstrap useEffect runs once (good), but something in the initialization chain never completes:
- Either fetchRequests() is waiting on a backend that doesn't respond
- Or the authReady state logic has wrong conditions
- Or session state never gets set properly

FIX STRATEGY:
Remove all async session/auth complexity. For a demo dashboard, we don't need real authentication.

COMPLETE IMPLEMENTATION:

1. REMOVE all session/auth logic from the component:
   - Remove useState for session, authReady, publicView
   - Remove the entire useEffect that bootstraps session
   - Remove any conditional rendering based on authReady

2. HARDCODE the demo state:
   - Set session to a mock Airbnb account object
   - Set requests to mock verification request data
   - No async fetching, no API calls

3. STRUCTURE the component like this:
```tsx
'use client';

import { useState } from 'react';
import { Shield, CheckCircle, Clock, XCircle, /* other icons */ } from 'lucide-react';

// Mock data

  company: 'Airbnb',
  email: 'verify@airbnb.com'
};

const MOCK_REQUESTS = [
  {
    id: '1',
    userName: 'Jordan Chen',
    timestamp: '2026-04-05T10:30:00',
    status: 'pending',
    attributes: ['full_name', 'date_of_birth', 'address'],
    attestation: {/* mock attestation data */}
  },
  // Add 2-3 more mock requests
];

export default function BusinessDashboard() {
  const [requests] = useState(MOCK_REQUESTS);
  const [selectedRequest, setSelectedRequest] = useState(null);

  return (
    <div className="min-h-screen bg-slate-50">
      {/* Header */}
      <header className="bg-white border-b border-slate-200">
        <div className="max-w-7xl mx-auto px-6 py-4 flex items-center justify-between">
          <div className="flex items-center gap-3">
            <Shield className="w-6 h-6 text-green-600" />
            <span className="text-xl font-semibold text-slate-900">Dokimos for Business</span>
          </div>
          
          {/* Viewing as indicator */}
          <div className="text-sm text-slate-600">
            Viewing as: <span className="font-semibold text-slate-900">{MOCK_SESSION.company}</span>
          </div>
        </div>
      </header>

      {/* Main content */}
      <main className="max-w-7xl mx-auto px-6 py-8">
        {/* Rest of dashboard UI - stats, verification cards, etc. */}
        {/* Use the existing dashboard components but feed them mock data */}
      </main>
    </div>
  );
}
```

Replace the entire page.tsx with this structure:
```tsx
'use client';

import { useState } from 'react';
import { Shield, CheckCircle, Clock, XCircle, FileText, ExternalLink } from 'lucide-react';

// Mock data
const MOCK_SESSION = {
  company: 'Airbnb',
  email: 'verify@airbnb.com'
};

const MOCK_REQUESTS = [
  {
    id: '1',
    userName: 'Jordan Chen',
    userEmail: 'jordan.chen.0@example.com',
    timestamp: '2026-04-03T10:30:00',
    status: 'approved',
    workflow: 'host_verification',
    attributeCount: 4,
    attributes: ['full_name', 'date_of_birth', 'address', 'government_id']
  },
  {
    id: '2',
    userName: 'Sarah Wilson',
    userEmail: 'sarah.wilson.1@example.com',
    timestamp: '2026-04-04T14:20:00',
    status: 'pending',
    workflow: 'driver_onboarding',
    attributeCount: 3,
    attributes: ['full_name', 'drivers_license', 'age_over_21']
  },
  {
    id: '3',
    userName: 'Michael Park',
    userEmail: 'michael.park.2@example.com',
    timestamp: '2026-04-04T16:45:00',
    status: 'approved',
    workflow: 'host_verification',
    attributeCount: 3,
    attributes: ['full_name', 'address', 'phone_number']
  },
  {
    id: '4',
    userName: 'Emma Rodriguez',
    userEmail: 'emma.rodriguez.3@example.com',
    timestamp: '2026-04-03T09:15:00',
    status: 'denied',
    workflow: 'continuous_driver_monitoring',
    attributeCount: 2,
    attributes: ['drivers_license', 'background_check']
  }
];

export default function BusinessDashboard() {
  const [requests] = useState(MOCK_REQUESTS);

  const getStatusBadge = (status: string) => {
    const styles = {
      approved: 'bg-green-100 text-green-700',
      pending: 'bg-amber-100 text-amber-700',
      denied: 'bg-red-100 text-red-700'
    };
    
    const icons = {
      approved: <CheckCircle className="w-3.5 h-3.5" />,
      pending: <Clock className="w-3.5 h-3.5" />,
      denied: <XCircle className="w-3.5 h-3.5" />
    };

    return (
      <span className={`inline-flex items-center gap-1 px-2 py-0.5 rounded text-xs font-semibold ${styles[status]}`}>
        {icons[status]}
        {status.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + status.slice(1)}
      </span>
    );
  };

  return (
    <div className="min-h-screen bg-slate-50">
      {/* Header */}
      <header className="bg-white border-b border-slate-200">
        <div className="max-w-7xl mx-auto px-6 py-4 flex items-center justify-between">
          <div className="flex items-center gap-3">
            <Shield className="w-6 h-6 text-green-600" />
            <span className="text-xl font-semibold text-slate-900">Dokimos for Business</span>
          </div>
          
          <div className="text-sm text-slate-600">
            Viewing as: <span className="font-semibold text-slate-900">{MOCK_SESSION.company}</span>
          </div>
        </div>
      </header>

      {/* Main Content */}
      <main className="max-w-7xl mx-auto px-6 py-8">
        {/* How it Works Section */}
        <div className="mb-8 bg-white border border-slate-200 rounded-xl p-6">
          <h2 className="text-lg font-semibold text-slate-900 mb-4">How Dokimos Works</h2>
          <div className="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-4 gap-6">
            <div>
              <div className="text-2xl font-bold text-slate-900 mb-1">1</div>
              <div className="text-sm text-slate-600">Document processed in Intel TDX secure enclave</div>
            </div>
            <div>
              <div className="text-2xl font-bold text-slate-900 mb-1">2</div>
              <div className="text-sm text-slate-600">TEE generates attestation</div>
              <div className="text-xs text-slate-500 mt-1">Cryptographic proof of code execution (MRENCLAVE)</div>
            </div>
            <div>
              <div className="text-2xl font-bold text-slate-900 mb-1">3</div>
              <div className="text-sm text-slate-600">Eigen AVS verifies attestation</div>
              <div className="text-xs text-slate-500 mt-1">Economic security via operator staking</div>
            </div>
            <div>
              <div className="text-2xl font-bold text-slate-900 mb-1">4</div>
              <div className="text-sm text-slate-600">You verify independently</div>
              <div className="text-xs text-slate-500 mt-1">Check signature on Etherscan, deployment on EigenCloud</div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        {/* Recent Activity */}
        <div className="mb-4 flex items-center justify-between">
          <h2 className="text-lg font-semibold text-slate-900">Recent activity</h2>
          <button className="text-sm text-green-600 hover:text-green-700 font-medium">
            View all verifications →
          </button>
        </div>

        {/* Verification Cards */}
        <div className="space-y-4">
          {requests.map((request) => (
            <div key={request.id} className="bg-white border border-slate-200 rounded-xl p-6 hover:border-slate-300 transition-colors">
              <div className="flex items-start justify-between mb-4">
                <div>
                  <div className="flex items-center gap-2 mb-1">
                    {getStatusBadge(request.status)}
                    <span className="text-sm text-slate-600">{request.userEmail}</span>
                  </div>
                  <div className="text-xs text-slate-500">
                    {new Date(request.timestamp).toLocaleDateString('en-US', { 
                      month: 'numeric', 
                      day: 'numeric', 
                      year: 'numeric' 
                    })}
                  </div>
                </div>
              </div>

              <div className="text-sm text-slate-600 mb-2">
                {request.workflow.replace(/_/g, ' ')} · {request.attributeCount} attributes
              </div>
            </div>
          ))}
        </div>
      </main>
    </div>
  );
}
```

3:22 PM
Perfect! You're back to a stable dashboard. Now let's systematically fix the Verifications tab. I'll tackle each problem with the PM feedback → Design feedback → Complete fix pattern.

Problem 1: Unrealistic Names and Emails
PM Feedback
Fake-looking data kills credibility in demos. "Michael park 2" reads like Lorem Ipsum — it signals "this is a placeholder" not "this is a real product." When Gaj sees michael.park.2@example.com, his brain registers "demo data" and discounts everything else.

Real names make the demo feel lived-in. "Sarah Chen" with sarah.chen@gmail.com makes him think "oh, these are actual verification requests" even though he knows it's mock data. The psychological difference is huge.

Design Feedback
The naming pattern reveals lazy data generation:

"Michael park 2" — lowercase, space before number
"Sarah wilson 1" — inconsistent capitalization
Numbers appended like database IDs
Real people don't have their emails formatted like firstname.lastname.NUMBER@example.com. That's a generated pattern.

Professional mock data:

Proper name capitalization: "Sarah Chen", "Jordan Williams"
Realistic email domains: Gmail, Outlook, company domains
No sequential numbering
The Fix
Replace all mock user data with realistic names and emails:

MOCK_REQUESTS array should have:

[
  {
    id: '1',
    userName: 'Michael Park',  // Proper case
    userEmail: 'mpark94@gmail.com',  // Realistic Gmail
    timestamp: '2026-04-05T15:19:00',
    status: 'verified',
    workflow: 'Host Verification',
    faceMatch: 94,
    attributes: ['full_name', 'date_of_birth', 'address', 'government_id']
  },
  {
    id: '2',
    userName: 'Sarah Wilson',
    userEmail: 'swilson@outlook.com',  // Different provider
    timestamp: '2026-04-04T15:19:00',
    status: 'pending',
    workflow: 'Driver Onboarding',
    faceMatch: null,  // No match yet for pending
    attributes: ['full_name', 'drivers_license', 'age_over_21']
  },
  {
    id: '3',
    userName: 'Jordan Chen',
    userEmail: 'jchen@gmail.com',
    timestamp: '2026-04-03T15:19:00',
    status: 'verified',
    workflow: 'Host Verification',
    faceMatch: 94,
    attributes: ['full_name', 'address', 'phone_number']
  },
  {
    id: '4',
    userName: 'Emma Rodriguez',
    userEmail: 'e.rodriguez@yahoo.com',  // Another provider
    timestamp: '2026-04-03T09:15:00',
    status: 'denied',
    workflow: 'Continuous Driver Monitoring',
    faceMatch: null,  // No match on denied
    attributes: ['drivers_license', 'background_check']
  }
]

Rules:
- All names in Title Case: "Sarah Wilson" not "sarah wilson 1"
- Realistic email patterns: mpark94@gmail.com, not michael.park.2@example.com
- Mix providers: Gmail, Outlook, Yahoo
- Remove all sequential numbering
Problem 2: Status Badges with Emojis
PM Feedback
Emojis in professional dashboards scream "consumer app" or "prototype." Stripe doesn't use ✓ emojis in status badges. Linear doesn't. Notion doesn't.

Status should be communicated through color + text, not icons. The color is enough — green background = verified, amber = pending, red = denied. Adding checkmark emojis is redundant and childish.

Design Feedback
Current badges likely look like:

✓ Verified  (green badge)
⏱ Pending   (amber badge)
✗ Denied    (red badge)
Problems:

Emoji competes with color for attention
Breaks visual rhythm (different widths)
Looks unprofessional
Professional pattern:

Just text + background color
Consistent badge width
Clean typography
The Fix
Remove all emojis from status badges:

In the status rendering logic, replace:

// OLD (with emojis)
const statusConfig = {
  verified: { emoji: '✓', color: 'bg-green-100 text-green-700' },
  pending: { emoji: '⏱', color: 'bg-amber-100 text-amber-700' },
  denied: { emoji: '✗', color: 'bg-red-100 text-red-700' }
};

// NEW (clean)
const statusConfig = {
  verified: { color: 'bg-green-100 text-green-700', text: 'Verified' },
  pending: { color: 'bg-amber-100 text-amber-700', text: 'Pending' },
  denied: { color: 'bg-red-100 text-red-700', text: 'Denied' }
};

Status badge component should render as:
<span className={`px-2 py-1 rounded text-xs font-semibold ${statusConfig[status].color}`}>
  {statusConfig[status].text}
</span>

No icons, no emojis. Just clean text.
Problem 3: Table Headers Inconsistent Formatting
PM Feedback
Column headers communicate information hierarchy. When they're inconsistent (some uppercase, some mixed case, different alignment), users have to work harder to parse the table.

Professional data tables use:

All caps for headers (VERIFIED, STATUS, ACTIONS)
Consistent spacing
Clear sort affordances (arrows that look clickable)
Design Feedback
Current headers are probably:

Name ↕  Email  Workflow ↕  Status ↕  Verified ↕  FACE MATCH  ACTIONS
Problems:

Inconsistent capitalization (mix of title case and caps)
Sort arrows are misaligned or unclear
"Verified" as both a header and a status is confusing
Headers don't look like headers
Professional pattern:

NAME        EMAIL        WORKFLOW        STATUS        VERIFIED        FACE MATCH    ACTIONS
All uppercase
Consistent spacing
Clear column purpose
The Fix
Standardize all table headers:

Column headers should ALL be uppercase:
- NAME (not "Name")
- EMAIL (not "Email")  
- WORKFLOW (not "Workflow")
- STATUS (not "Status")
- VERIFIED (timestamp, not status — rename to "DATE VERIFIED")
- FACE MATCH (not "Face Match")
- ACTIONS (not "Actions")

Styling for headers:
className="text-xs font-semibold text-slate-600 uppercase tracking-wider"

Sort indicators:
- Use lucide-react's ArrowUpDown icon
- Only show on sortable columns (Name, Workflow, Status, Date Verified)
- Icon should be text-slate-400 and small (w-3 h-3)
- Position: inline after text with small gap

Example header:
<th className="px-4 py-3 text-left text-xs font-semibold text-slate-600 uppercase tracking-wider">
  <div className="flex items-center gap-1">
    NAME
    <ArrowUpDown className="w-3 h-3 text-slate-400" />
  </div>
</th>

Rename "VERIFIED" column to "DATE VERIFIED" to avoid confusion with status.
Problem 4: Face Match Percentage Unclear Value
PM Feedback
You're questioning whether face match % adds value. Good instinct — if you're not sure why a metric exists, users won't be either.

Ask: "What decision does this number inform?"

If face match is 94%, what do I do differently than if it's 87%?
Is there a threshold below which I should investigate?
Do I have controls to adjust sensitivity?
If the answer is "no" or "I don't know," the metric is vanity data.

Two options: A) Keep it but make it actionable — show threshold ("94% • Above required 85%") B) Remove it — if it's just "looks good"

Design Feedback
The column takes up valuable horizontal space. If it's staying, it needs purpose.

If keeping:

Show threshold context
Use color to indicate pass/fail
Make it scannable
If removing:

Face match can still be in the detailed view when clicking "View"
Table gets simpler, more scannable
The Fix
OPTION A: Keep face match with context

Add threshold indicator:
<div className="text-sm text-slate-900">
  {request.faceMatch}%
  {request.faceMatch >= 85 && (
    <span className="ml-1 text-xs text-green-600">✓</span>
  )}
</div>

Or with colored background:
{request.faceMatch >= 85 ? (
  <span className="text-sm font-medium text-green-700">{request.faceMatch}%</span>
) : (
  <span className="text-sm font-medium text-amber-700">{request.faceMatch}%</span>
)}

OPTION B: Remove from table, show in detail view

Remove the FACE MATCH column entirely from table.
When user clicks "View" on a row, show face match in the detail panel.

I recommend OPTION B — cleaner table, detail is available when needed.
Problem 5: Missing "Verify Independently" Link
PM Feedback
This is the most important element you're missing.

The entire value prop of Dokimos is cryptographic verifiability. Without the "verify independently" step, you're showing Persona with extra steps. Gaj and Soubhik NEED to see this — it's what makes EigenCompute matter.

The three-step progressive disclosure should be:

See the verification request (table view)
View the attestation (detail modal)
Verify independently (link to Eigen dashboard + Etherscan)
Step 3 is where the magic happens — where they see this isn't "trust Pranavi's server," it's "verify the TEE signature onchain."

Design Feedback
The "View" button probably opens a modal with attestation data, but no clear path to verify it independently.

What's needed:

Button or link labeled "Verify Independently"
Opens the Eigen verifiability dashboard
Shows Etherscan signature verification link
Maybe includes the wallet address prominently
The Fix
Add "Verify Independently" section to verification detail view:

When user clicks "View" on a table row, show detail modal with three sections:

1. VERIFICATION DETAILS
   - User: {name}
   - Status: {status badge}
   - Workflow: {workflow}
   - Attributes verified: {list}

2. CRYPTOGRAPHIC ATTESTATION
   - TEE Wallet: 0x2E5100a47aE27a71F389bce2588cB2473b94d2d9
   - Signature: {truncated hash}
   - [Copy full attestation] button

3. VERIFY INDEPENDENTLY (this is new)
   Add a prominent section:

   <div className="mt-6 p-4 bg-slate-50 border border-slate-200 rounded-lg">
     <div className="flex items-start gap-3">
       <Shield className="w-5 h-5 text-green-600 mt-0.5" />

         <div className="text-sm font-semibold text-slate-900 mb-2">
           Verify this attestation independently
         </div>
         <div className="text-sm text-slate-600 mb-3">
           Don't trust us — verify the cryptographic proof yourself:
         </div>
         <div className="space-y-2">
           <a 
             href={`https://sepolia.etherscan.io/verifySig/...`}
             target="_blank"
             className="flex items-center gap-2 text-sm text-green-600 hover:text-green-700"
           >
             <ExternalLink className="w-4 h-4" />
             Verify signature on Etherscan
           </a>
           <a 
             href={`https://console.eigencloud.com/verifiability/...`}
             target="_blank"
             className="flex items-center gap-2 text-sm text-green-600 hover:text-green-700"
           >
             <ExternalLink className="w-4 h-4" />
             Check TEE deployment on EigenCloud
           </a>
         </div>
       </div>
     </div>
   </div>

Make this section PROMINENT. It's the entire point of the demo.

Perfect! I found it. Now let me create the comprehensive prompt for you based on that original progressive disclosure design. This was built for the verifier side (business dashboard), and it's the three-layer approach that walks non-technical users through verification.

Complete Progressive Disclosure Implementation Prompt
PM Context
Purpose: Build a three-layer progressive disclosure UI for the Verifications tab that walks business users (Airbnb, Uber compliance teams) through attestation verification—from simple verdict to cryptographic proof.

User journey:

User clicks "View" on a verification request in the table
Layer 1 appears: Simple yes/no answer with what was verified
User clicks "How does this work?" → Layer 2: Plain-language explainer
User clicks "Verify It Yourself" → Layer 3: Technical step-by-step verification
Key principle: Meet users where they are—most people need Layer 1, curious users want Layer 2, technical/compliance teams need Layer 3.

Design Specifications
Layer 1: Simple Verdict (Everyone)
Visual structure:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ✓ Verification Confirmed                       │
│                                                  │
│  Jordan Lee's identity has been verified by     │
│  secure hardware. No manual review needed.      │
│                                                  │
│  Verified: Apr 5, 2026 at 2:34 PM               │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│  │  What was verified:                  │      │
│  │  ✓ Full Name: Jordan Lee             │      │
│  │  ✓ Age: Over 18 years old            │      │
│  │  ✓ ID Document: Valid until 2028     │      │
│  │  ✓ Photo Match: Face matches ID (94%)│      │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│                                                  │
│  [How does this work?]  [Technical Details]    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Key elements:

Big green checkmark (CheckCircle from lucide-react)
Simple headline: "Verification Confirmed"
Plain language explanation (no jargon)
Timestamp
List of verified attributes with checkmarks
Two clear navigation paths forward
Styling:

White background
Green accent for checkmark (
#10B981)
Slate text (
#0F172A for headlines, 
#64748B for body)
Rounded corners (rounded-xl)
Padding: p-6
Inner box: bg-slate-50 with subtle border
Layer 2: How It Works (Curious Users)
Triggered by: Click "How does this work?"

Visual structure:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  How Dokimos Verification Works                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                  │
│  Think of Dokimos like a notary, but digital    │
│  and impossible to fake.                        │
│                                                  │
│  Here's what happened:                          │
│                                                  │
│  1️⃣ Jordan uploaded her ID once                 │
│     → Sent to secure, isolated hardware         │
│                                                  │
│  2️⃣ The hardware checked:                       │
│     • Is the ID real and not expired?           │
│     • Does her face match the ID photo?         │
│     • Is she over 18?                           │
│                                                  │
│  3️⃣ The hardware created a digital proof        │
│     → Signed with cryptography (like a          │
│       tamper-proof seal)                        │
│                                                  │
│  4️⃣ You received that proof                     │
│     → You can verify it's real, independent     │
│       of Dokimos                                │
│                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│  │  Why this matters:                   │      │
│  │                                       │      │
│  │  • No manual review needed           │      │
│  │  • Can't be faked or tampered with   │      │
│  │  • Jordan never uploads her ID again │      │
│  │  • Complete audit trail              │      │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│                                                  │
│  [← Back]  [Verify It Yourself →]              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Key elements:

Analogy first ("like a notary")
Numbered steps (emoji numbers for visual hierarchy)
Bullet points for sub-steps
Benefits callout box
Two navigation buttons (Back to Layer 1, Forward to Layer 3)
Styling:

Same white background
Steps use larger text (text-base)
Sub-bullets indented with arrow (→)
Benefits box: bg-green-50 border-l-4 border-green-600
Navigation buttons: text buttons, not filled
Layer 3: Verify It Yourself (Technical/Compliance)
Triggered by: Click "Verify It Yourself" or "Technical Details"

Visual structure:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Verify This Proof Yourself                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                  │
│  Follow these steps to independently verify     │
│  this proof. No technical expertise required.   │
│                                                  │
│  Step 1 of 5: Check the Digital Signature       │
│  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  20%                    │
│                                                  │
│  ✓ Signature is valid                           │
│                                                  │
│  ▶ What this proves                             │
│  │                                               │
│  │ The verification result came from Dokimos's  │
│  │ secure hardware and hasn't been tampered     │
│  │ with. Think of it like a wax seal on an      │
│  │ important document—if the seal is intact,    │
│  │ you know it's authentic.                     │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────│
│                                                  │
│  Signature: 0x8f3c92a4b7e1d5f6...              │
│  Signer: 0x2E5100a47aE27a71...                 │
│                                                  │
│  [Verify on Etherscan →]                        │
│                                                  │
│  ────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                  │
│  Step 2 of 5: Verify the Hardware               │
│  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  40%                    │
│                                                  │
│  ✓ Ran on Intel TDX secure hardware             │
│  ✓ Production mode (not testing)                │
│                                                  │
│  ▽ What this proves (click to expand)           │
│                                                  │
│  TEE Platform: Intel TDX                         │
│  Enclave ID: 0xa7f3c89b...                      │
│  Debug Mode: Disabled                           │
│                                                  │
│  [View on EigenCloud Dashboard →]               │
│                                                  │
│  ────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                  │
│  Step 3 of 5: Check Source Code                 │
│  Step 4 of 5: Verify Build Provenance           │
│  Step 5 of 5: Confirm Face Match                │
│                                                  │
│  [← Back to Summary]                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Key elements:

Progress indicator (Step X of 5 + progress bar)
Each step has:
Title
Status indicators (✓ checkmarks)
Expandable "What this proves" section (▶/▽)
Relevant data fields
External verification link
Divider between steps
Back button at bottom
Styling:

Progress bar: bg-green-600 with bg-slate-200 track
Status checkmarks: text-green-600
Expandable sections: toggle icon rotates, content slides
Code/hashes: font-mono text-xs text-slate-600
External links: text-green-600 with ExternalLink icon
Complete Cursor Prompt
Build a three-layer progressive disclosure UI for verifying attestations in the business dashboard. This will be triggered when clicking "View" on a verification request in the Verifications table.

CONTEXT:
- File: dokimos-app-v2/src/app/business/page.tsx (or create new component)
- Triggered from: Verifications table "View" button
- Audience: Business users (Airbnb compliance team, Uber background check managers)
- Goal: Walk from simple verdict → plain explanation → technical proof

DESIGN SYSTEM:
- Colors: Green accents (#10B981), slate text (#0F172A headlines, #64748B body)
- Typography: Geist font family
- Spacing: p-6 for cards, gap-4 between elements
- Corners: rounded-xl
- Icons: lucide-react (CheckCircle, ExternalLink, ChevronRight, ChevronDown)

COMPONENT STRUCTURE:

Create VerificationModal component with state for current layer:
```tsx
'use client';

import { useState } from 'react';
import { CheckCircle, ExternalLink, ChevronRight, ChevronDown, X } from 'lucide-react';

interface VerificationData {
  userName: string;
  verifiedAt: string;
  attributes: {
    label: string;
    value: string;
  }[];
  attestation: {
    signature: string;
    signer: string;
    messageHash: string;
    teeInfo: {
      platform: string;
      enclaveId: string;
      debugMode: boolean;
    };
  };
}

export function VerificationModal({ data, onClose }: { data: VerificationData; onClose: () => void }) {
  const [layer, setLayer] = useState(1);
  const [expandedSteps, setExpandedSteps] = useState<number[]>([]);

  return (
    <div className="fixed inset-0 bg-black/50 flex items-center justify-center z-50">
      <div className="bg-white rounded-xl max-w-2xl w-full max-h-[90vh] overflow-y-auto">
        {/* Close button */}
        <div className="sticky top-0 bg-white border-b border-slate-200 p-4 flex justify-end">
          <button onClick={onClose} className="text-slate-400 hover:text-slate-600">
            <X className="w-5 h-5" />
          </button>
        </div>

        {/* Layer content */}
        <div className="p-6">
          {layer === 1 && <Layer1SimpleVerdict data={data} onNext={() => setLayer(2)} onTechnical={() => setLayer(3)} />}
          {layer === 2 && <Layer2HowItWorks onBack={() => setLayer(1)} onNext={() => setLayer(3)} />}
          {layer === 3 && <Layer3Technical data={data} expandedSteps={expandedSteps} setExpandedSteps={setExpandedSteps} onBack={() => setLayer(1)} />}
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}
```

LAYER 1 COMPONENT:
```tsx
function Layer1SimpleVerdict({ data, onNext, onTechnical }) {
  return (
    <div className="space-y-6">
      {/* Header */}
      <div className="flex items-start gap-3">
        <CheckCircle className="w-8 h-8 text-green-600 flex-shrink-0 mt-1" />
        <div>
          <h2 className="text-2xl font-semibold text-slate-900">Verification Confirmed</h2>
          <p className="text-slate-600 mt-2">
            {data.userName}'s identity has been verified by secure hardware. No manual review needed.
          </p>
        </div>
      </div>

      {/* Timestamp */}
      <div className="text-sm text-slate-600">
        Verified: {new Date(data.verifiedAt).toLocaleString('en-US', {
          month: 'short',
          day: 'numeric',
          year: 'numeric',
          hour: 'numeric',
          minute: '2-digit',
          hour12: true
        })}
      </div>

      {/* Attributes box */}
      <div className="bg-slate-50 border border-slate-200 rounded-lg p-4">
        <h3 className="text-sm font-semibold text-slate-900 mb-3">What was verified:</h3>
        <div className="space-y-2">
          {data.attributes.map((attr, i) => (
            <div key={i} className="flex items-start gap-2 text-sm">
              <CheckCircle className="w-4 h-4 text-green-600 flex-shrink-0 mt-0.5" />
              <div>
                <span className="font-medium text-slate-900">{attr.label}:</span>{' '}
                <span className="text-slate-600">{attr.value}</span>
              </div>
            </div>
          ))}
        </div>
      </div>

      {/* Navigation */}
      <div className="flex gap-3">
        <button
          onClick={onNext}
          className="flex-1 px-4 py-2 text-sm font-medium text-green-600 hover:text-green-700 hover:bg-green-50 rounded-lg transition-colors"
        >
          How does this work?
        </button>
        <button
          onClick={onTechnical}
          className="flex-1 px-4 py-2 text-sm font-medium text-slate-600 hover:text-slate-900 hover:bg-slate-100 rounded-lg transition-colors"
        >
          Technical Details
        </button>
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}
```

LAYER 2 COMPONENT:
```tsx
function Layer2HowItWorks({ onBack, onNext }) {
  return (
    <div className="space-y-6">
      {/* Header */}
      <h2 className="text-2xl font-semibold text-slate-900">How Dokimos Verification Works</h2>

      {/* Analogy */}
      <p className="text-slate-600">
        Think of Dokimos like a notary, but digital and impossible to fake.
      </p>

      {/* Steps */}
      <div className="space-y-4">
        <p className="text-sm font-medium text-slate-900">Here's what happened:</p>

        {/* Step 1 */}
        <div className="flex gap-3">
          <div className="flex-shrink-0 w-8 h-8 rounded-full bg-green-100 text-green-700 flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-semibold">
            1
          </div>
          <div>
            <p className="text-sm font-medium text-slate-900">Jordan uploaded her ID once</p>
            <p className="text-sm text-slate-600 mt-1">→ Sent to secure, isolated hardware</p>
          </div>
        </div>

        {/* Step 2 */}
        <div className="flex gap-3">
          <div className="flex-shrink-0 w-8 h-8 rounded-full bg-green-100 text-green-700 flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-semibold">
            2
          </div>
          <div>
            <p className="text-sm font-medium text-slate-900">The hardware checked:</p>
            <ul className="text-sm text-slate-600 mt-1 space-y-1">
              <li>• Is the ID real and not expired?</li>
              <li>• Does her face match the ID photo?</li>
              <li>• Is she over 18?</li>
            </ul>
          </div>
        </div>

        {/* Step 3 */}
        <div className="flex gap-3">
          <div className="flex-shrink-0 w-8 h-8 rounded-full bg-green-100 text-green-700 flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-semibold">
            3
          </div>
          <div>
            <p className="text-sm font-medium text-slate-900">The hardware created a digital proof</p>
            <p className="text-sm text-slate-600 mt-1">→ Signed with cryptography (like a tamper-proof seal)</p>
          </div>
        </div>

        {/* Step 4 */}
        <div className="flex gap-3">
          <div className="flex-shrink-0 w-8 h-8 rounded-full bg-green-100 text-green-700 flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-semibold">
            4
          </div>
          <div>
            <p className="text-sm font-medium text-slate-900">You received that proof</p>
            <p className="text-sm text-slate-600 mt-1">→ You can verify it's real, independent of Dokimos</p>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>

      {/* Benefits callout */}
      <div className="bg-green-50 border-l-4 border-green-600 p-4 rounded">
        <p className="text-sm font-semibold text-slate-900 mb-2">Why this matters:</p>
        <ul className="text-sm text-slate-700 space-y-1">
          <li>• No manual review needed</li>
          <li>• Can't be faked or tampered with</li>
          <li>• Jordan never uploads her ID again</li>
          <li>• Complete audit trail</li>
        </ul>
      </div>

      {/* Navigation */}
      <div className="flex gap-3">
        <button
          onClick={onBack}
          className="px-4 py-2 text-sm font-medium text-slate-600 hover:text-slate-900 hover:bg-slate-100 rounded-lg transition-colors"
        >
          ← Back
        </button>
        <button
          onClick={onNext}
          className="flex-1 px-4 py-2 text-sm font-medium text-green-600 hover:text-green-700 hover:bg-green-50 rounded-lg transition-colors"
        >
          Verify It Yourself →
        </button>
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}
```

LAYER 3 COMPONENT:
```tsx
function Layer3Technical({ data, expandedSteps, setExpandedSteps, onBack }) {
  const toggleStep = (stepNum: number) => {
    setExpandedSteps(prev =>
      prev.includes(stepNum)
        ? prev.filter(n => n !== stepNum)
        : [...prev, stepNum]
    );
  };

  const steps = [
    {
      title: "Check the Digital Signature",
      progress: 20,
      status: "valid",
      proof: "The verification result came from Dokimos's secure hardware and hasn't been tampered with. Think of it like a wax seal on an important document—if the seal is intact, you know it's authentic.",
      details: [
        { label: "Signature", value: data.attestation.signature },
        { label: "Signer", value: data.attestation.signer },
        { label: "Message Hash", value: data.attestation.messageHash }
      ],
      link: {
        text: "Verify on Etherscan",
        url: `https://sepolia.etherscan.io/verifiedSignatures?a=${data.attestation.signer}`
      }
    },
    {
      title: "Verify the Hardware",
      progress: 40,
      status: "valid",
      proof: "The verification happened in isolated, tamper-proof hardware—not on a regular server that could be manipulated.",
      details: [
        { label: "TEE Platform", value: data.attestation.teeInfo.platform },
        { label: "Enclave ID", value: data.attestation.teeInfo.enclaveId },
        { label: "Debug Mode", value: data.attestation.teeInfo.debugMode ? "Enabled" : "Disabled" }
      ],
      link: {
        text: "View on EigenCloud Dashboard",
        url: "https://verify-sepolia.eigencloud.xyz/app/0x5911a27103C4de497fCB5C00D8e19962EEF0008E"
      }
    },
    // Add remaining steps...
  ];

  return (
    <div className="space-y-6">
      {/* Header */}
      <div>
        <h2 className="text-2xl font-semibold text-slate-900">Verify This Proof Yourself</h2>
        <p className="text-slate-600 mt-2">
          Follow these steps to independently verify this proof. No technical expertise required.
        </p>
      </div>

      {/* Steps */}
      <div className="space-y-6">
        {steps.map((step, i) => (
          <div key={i}>
            {/* Step header */}
            <div className="flex items-center justify-between mb-2">
              <p className="text-sm font-semibold text-slate-900">Step {i + 1} of {steps.length}: {step.title}</p>
              <span className="text-xs text-slate-500">{step.progress}%</span>
            </div>

            {/* Progress bar */}
            <div className="h-1 bg-slate-200 rounded-full mb-4">
              <div
                className="h-full bg-green-600 rounded-full transition-all"
                style={{ width: `${step.progress}%` }}
              />
            </div>

            {/* Status */}
            <div className="flex items-center gap-2 mb-3">
              <CheckCircle className="w-4 h-4 text-green-600" />
              <span className="text-sm text-green-700 font-medium">Signature is valid</span>
            </div>

            {/* What this proves (expandable) */}
            <button
              onClick={() => toggleStep(i)}
              className="flex items-center gap-2 text-sm text-slate-600 hover:text-slate-900 mb-3"
            >
              {expandedSteps.includes(i) ? (
                <ChevronDown className="w-4 h-4" />
              ) : (
                <ChevronRight className="w-4 h-4" />
              )}
              What this proves
            </button>

            {expandedSteps.includes(i) && (
              <div className="ml-6 mb-4 p-3 bg-slate-50 border-l-2 border-slate-300 rounded text-sm text-slate-600">
                {step.proof}
              </div>
            )}

            {/* Details */}
            <div className="space-y-2 mb-4">
              {step.details.map((detail, j) => (
                <div key={j} className="flex items-start gap-2 text-xs">
                  <span className="text-slate-500 min-w-[100px]">{detail.label}:</span>
                  <code className="font-mono text-slate-700 break-all">{detail.value}</code>
                </div>
              ))}
            </div>

            {/* External link */}
            
              href={step.link.url}
              target="_blank"
              rel="noopener noreferrer"
              className="inline-flex items-center gap-2 text-sm text-green-600 hover:text-green-700"
            >
              {step.link.text}
              <ExternalLink className="w-4 h-4" />
            </a>

            {/* Divider */}
            {i < steps.length - 1 && <div className="border-t border-slate-200 mt-6" />}
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>

      {/* Back button */}
      <button
        onClick={onBack}
        className="px-4 py-2 text-sm font-medium text-slate-600 hover:text-slate-900 hover:bg-slate-100 rounded-lg transition-colors"
      >
        ← Back to Summary
      </button>
    </div>
  );
}
```

const [selectedVerification, setSelectedVerification] = useState(null);

// In table row:

  onClick={() => setSelectedVerification(request)}
  className="text-sm text-green-600 hover:text-green-700 font-medium"
>
  View
</button>

// At end of component:

  <VerificationModal
    data={selectedVerification}
    onClose={() => setSelectedVerification(null)}
  />
)}

The "Create workflow" modal is excellent. Clean, clear, professional. It does exactly what it needs to do — lets business users define what they want verified without touching code.

The Programs page has good bones but needs refinement. Here's what's working and what's not:

What's Working ✅
Clear value prop — "Create custom verification programs" tells users exactly what this page does
Stats are prominent — 1,247 / 89% / 3m 24s gives instant signal on program performance
Two-column "What gets verified" layout — scannable, organized
Compliance callout — shows business value (FCRA compliance, DMV regulations)
Action hierarchy is clear — "View verifications" is the primary CTA
What's Not Working ❌
1. The title "Verification programs" uses a serif font

Inconsistent with rest of app (rest is sans-serif)
Looks dated, less professional
Should be: text-2xl font-semibold text-slate-900 (Geist, not serif)
2. The icon is generic

Purple person icon doesn't communicate "driver background check"
Should use relevant lucide-react icons based on workflow type:
Driver Background Check → Car icon
Host Verification → Home icon
Vehicle Registration → FileText icon
3. Stats layout is functional but uninspired

Three equal boxes side-by-side is standard but flat
No visual hierarchy — all three metrics have same weight
"this month" / "approved" / "avg time" labels are too small

All caps is correct (you got this right)
But needs more visual separation from the content below
Font weight/size could be stronger
5. Compliance section lacks visual weight

Shield icon is good, but the text is too subdued
Should be more prominent — compliance is a key selling point
"FCRA compliance, State DMV regulations" reads like fine print
6. Button hierarchy could be clearer

"View verifications" (primary) vs "Edit program" (secondary) vs "API" (utility) all have similar visual weight
API dropdown feels tacked on

Problem 1: Title Typography Inconsistency
Current:

Verification programs  ← Serif font (probably Instrument Serif)

Your user-facing app uses Instrument Serif for one emotional moment per screen (like "The last time you'll upload your ID")

tsx

3:34 PM
PM Feedback First
You're absolutely right to question this copy. This is supposed to be the "accessible" layer for non-technical users, but it's still full of jargon. Let's test it against the actual audience:

Sarah Chen, Airbnb Compliance Manager, reads this:

"Sent to secure, isolated hardware" → What does that mean? Like a server?
"Signed with cryptography (like a tamper-proof seal)" → I don't know what cryptography is
"You can verify it's real, independent of Dokimos" → How? Where? What am I verifying?
She closes the modal. Nothing clicked.

The analogy "like a notary" is actually good — but then we immediately abandon it and go back to technical terms. We need to commit to the analogy and explain everything through it.

Design Feedback (Copy Problems)
Problem 1: The Analogy Isn't Strong Enough
Current:

"Think of Dokimos like a notary, but digital and impossible to fake."
Why it's weak:

"Digital and impossible to fake" is vague
Doesn't actually explain what makes it better than a regular notary
Doesn't connect to what the user cares about (speed, convenience, security)
Better options:

Option A (Emphasize speed + certainty):

"Dokimos works like a notary — but instant, cryptographically sealed, 
and verifiable by anyone."
Option B (Emphasize trust model):

"Think of Dokimos as a digital notary that no one — not even Dokimos — 
can tamper with."
Option C (Emphasize the user benefit):

"Dokimos is an automated identity check that gives you mathematical 
proof instead of asking you to trust us."
I recommend Option C — it explains the value (proof vs. trust) in plain language.

Problem 2: Technical Jargon Throughout
Let's fix each step one by one:

Step 1: "Sent to secure, isolated hardware"
Current:

Sarah uploaded their ID once
→ Sent to secure, isolated hardware
Problems:

"Hardware" is vague (a laptop is hardware)
"Isolated" doesn't mean anything to non-technical users
Doesn't explain WHY this matters
Better:

Sarah uploaded their ID once
→ Processed in a sealed digital vault that even Dokimos can't open
Or even simpler:

Sarah uploaded their ID once
→ Checked automatically by secure hardware (not by people)
Why this works:

"Sealed vault" is a familiar concept
"Even Dokimos can't open" explains the trust model
"Not by people" addresses the privacy concern
Step 2: "The hardware checked:"
Current:

The hardware checked:
- Is the ID real and not expired?
- Does their face match the ID photo?
- Are they old enough for this check?
Problems:

"The hardware" is too abstract
Questions are passive — doesn't show action
"Old enough for this check" is awkward
Better:

The system verified:
✓ ID is real and current
✓ Face matches the photo
✓ Meets age requirements
Or with more personality:

Three checks happened automatically:
✓ Is this a real, valid ID?
✓ Does the person match the photo?
✓ Do they meet the age requirement?
Step 3: "Signed with cryptography (like a tamper-proof seal)"
Current:

The hardware created a digital proof
→ Signed with cryptography (like a tamper-proof seal)
Problems:

"Cryptography" is jargon
The analogy is in parentheses (feels like an afterthought)
Doesn't explain what a "digital proof" actually is
Better:

A tamper-proof record was created
→ Like a notary's seal, but impossible to forge
Or:

The system created a signed certificate
→ Think of it like a notarized document that can't be faked
Why this works:

Leads with the analogy (notary seal, certificate)
"Impossible to forge" is the key benefit
No jargon
Step 4: "You can verify it's real, independent of Dokimos"
Current:

You received that proof
→ You can verify it's real, independent of Dokimos
Problems:

Second-person "you" is confusing (who is "you"? Sarah or Airbnb?)
"Independent of Dokimos" is abstract
Doesn't explain HOW to verify
Better:

You received the proof
→ Anyone can check it's legitimate — no need to trust Dokimos
Or more specific:

Airbnb received the proof
→ They can verify it's real using public blockchain records
Or simplest:

The proof was sent to Airbnb
→ They can verify it themselves — no trust required
Problem 3: Second-Person Language Is Confusing
The issue with "you":

In this context, who is "you"?

Is it Sarah (the user who uploaded her ID)?
Is it Airbnb (the verifier receiving the proof)?
Current text uses "you" for the verifier:

"You received that proof"
"You can verify it's real"
But earlier steps talk about Sarah in third person:

"Sarah uploaded their ID"
This is inconsistent and confusing.

Fix: Pick one POV and stick with it.

Option A: Third person throughout (clearest for explanation):

1. Sarah uploaded her ID once
   → Checked automatically by secure hardware

2. The system verified:
   ✓ ID is real and current
   ✓ Face matches the photo  
   ✓ Meets age requirements

3. A tamper-proof record was created
   → Like a notarized document that can't be faked

4. Airbnb received the proof
   → They can verify it themselves — no trust required
Option B: Second person from Airbnb's POV (more engaging):

1. Sarah uploaded her ID to Dokimos
   → It was checked automatically by secure hardware

2. The system verified:
   ✓ ID is real and current
   ✓ Face matches the photo
   ✓ Meets age requirements

3. A signed certificate was created
   → Like a notarized document, but impossible to forge

4. You received that certificate
   → You can verify it yourself using public records
I recommend Option A — clearest for an explanation layer.

Problem 4: "Why This Matters" Section Is Generic
Current:

Why this matters:
- No manual review needed
- Can't be faked or tampered with
- Users don't re-upload ID for every partner
- Complete audit trail
Problems:

Bullets are features, not benefits
"Complete audit trail" — so what?
Doesn't connect to the verifier's actual concerns
Better (focused on verifier benefits):

Why this matters for you:
- Instant verification — no waiting on manual review
- Fraud-proof — mathematically impossible to fake
- Lower liability — you never store raw ID documents
- Always verifiable — check the proof anytime, independently
Or with more urgency:

This changes everything:
- Verify in seconds, not days
- Zero risk of fake IDs slipping through
- No data breach liability (you never see the raw documents)
- Auditors can verify the proof themselves
Complete Rewritten Copy (Layer 2)
How Dokimos Verification Works

Dokimos is an automated identity check that gives you mathematical 
proof instead of asking you to trust us.

Here's what happened:

1  Sarah uploaded her ID to Dokimos
   → It was checked automatically by secure hardware (not by people)

2  The system verified:
   ✓ ID is real and current
   ✓ Face matches the photo
   ✓ Meets age requirements

3  A signed certificate was created
   → Like a notarized document, but impossible to forge

4  Airbnb received that certificate
   → They can verify it themselves using public blockchain records

───────────────────────────────────────

Why this matters for you:

- Instant verification — no waiting on manual review
- Fraud-proof — mathematically impossible to fake  
- Lower liability — you never store raw ID documents
- Always verifiable — check the proof anytime, independently

Design Feedback (Specific Copy Problems)
Problem 1: Opening Line Is Abstract
Current:

"Dokimos is an automated identity check that gives you mathematical 
proof instead of asking you to trust us."
Why it's still unclear:

"Mathematical proof" — what does that mean in practice?
Doesn't explain the physical thing we're doing
"Instead of asking you to trust us" — implies we're NOT trustworthy?
Better (focus on the concrete service):

"Dokimos verifies identities once, then creates a proof you can check 
yourself — so users never upload their ID again, and you never store 
raw documents."
Why this works:

Physical thing: "verifies identities" + "creates a proof"
Value for users: "never upload again"
Value for verifiers: "never store raw documents"
Trust mechanism: "you can check yourself"
Problem 2: Step 1 Needs Context, Not Jargon
Current:

Sarah uploaded her ID to Dokimos
→ Checked automatically by secure hardware (not by people)
Problems:

"Secure hardware" is vague tech-speak
"(not by people)" addresses privacy but doesn't explain HOW
Better (explain what actually happens):

Sarah uploaded her ID to Dokimos
→ Verified automatically in an isolated environment where no one — 
   not even Dokimos — can access the raw document
Or even clearer:

Sarah uploaded her ID once
→ It was processed in a sealed environment — Dokimos staff never 
   see the actual ID document
Why this works:

"Sealed environment" is concrete (like a locked room)
"Dokimos staff never see it" addresses the privacy concern directly
Explains the trust model without saying "hardware" or "TEE"
Problem 3: Step 3 Needs to Explain What the Certificate Enables
Current:

A signed certificate was created
→ Think of it like a notarized document that can't be faked
Why it's incomplete:

"Can't be faked" is good, but SO WHAT?
Doesn't explain what Airbnb can DO with this certificate
Doesn't connect to the final outcome (trust)
Better (explain what it enables):

A signed verification record was created
→ This record proves the checks happened and can be verified by 
   anyone using public blockchain records
Or more business-focused:

A cryptographically signed record was created
→ Airbnb can verify this independently — no need to trust 
   Dokimos's word
Or simplest (focus on the outcome):

A tamper-proof verification record was created
→ Airbnb receives proof they can check themselves, not just a 
   "yes/no" answer
Problem 4: Step 4 Needs to Close the Loop
Current:

Airbnb received that certificate
→ They can verify it themselves using public blockchain records
Why it's incomplete:

Ends with "blockchain records" (tech jargon)
Doesn't tie back to trust
Doesn't explain WHAT they're verifying
Better (close the trust loop):

Airbnb received the verification record
→ They can check it's legitimate at any time — the proof is 
   permanent and publicly verifiable
Or more direct:

You received the verification record
→ Check it yourself: the signature, the timestamp, and the checks 
   performed are all publicly verifiable

How Dokimos Verification Works

Dokimos verifies identities once, then creates a proof that 
organizations can check independently. You never have to 
store sensitive ID documents.

Here's what happened:

1  Sarah uploaded her ID to Dokimos

   It was verified automatically in an isolated hardware 
   environment where no one, not even Dokimos, can access 
   the raw document.

   and can be verified by anyone.

- Instant verification, no waiting
- Fraud-proof, mathematically impossible to fake

1775418949730_image.png
This is the pop-up that happens when you click "Learn more" from "What just happened", like the pop-up after a user first signs up. The thing here, though, is that I think we should change this text a lot. I don't like the four steps. I think "secured processing" is not a good one, and it's still very jargon-heavy. I like that your document is sent over an encrypted connection. Give context: what does it mean, because the connection is encrypted? You get proof.
I liked that you are not storing a copy of your ID for casual browsing, but I basically want you to remove all the em dashes in this as well. I also like the digital proof that your ID was verified. Don't say "partner"; say "organizations". I really like the proof of age without exposing your full birth date. That's really good.
At stations, I think is really technical. I think I wonder if you can replace that or give more context if you say that? I like this; I like the ending. You 
How Dokimos protects your identity
1
You upload your ID
Your document is sent over an encrypted connection to a secure processor.
2
Secured processing
A trusted execution environment (TEE) verifies your document and face match.
3
You get proof
You receive a credential you can use to approve requests — not a stored copy of your ID for casual browsing.
4
Share on your terms
Approve age or identity checks without re-uploading your document every time.
What happens to your ID?
Your ID is sent encrypted (like HTTPS) to a secure environment that can verify it and produce a signed result. The goal is verification — not keeping your document as an ordinary file for employees to open.
In simple terms, the system can:
* Read what it needs to verify
* Produce a cryptographic attestation you can share
* Follow a policy that limits what is retained after verification
What is a verified credential?
Instead of sending your physical ID image to every service, you can present a digital proof that your identity was verified — so partners can rely on the check without receiving a copy of your document.
Depending on the request, that can mean:
* Proof of age without exposing your full birthdate
* Proof the document was verified, not the raw document itself
* Proof tied to cryptography rather than “trust us”
How can I trust this?
Attestations can be published alongside details that allow independent verification — for example, what code ran and when. You can open the verification dashboard to inspect the EigenCompute application tied to this deployment.
View technical details
What Dokimos is designed not to do
The product goal is to avoid treating your ID like a normal file in a dashboard. That means minimizing unnecessary retention and sharing — you stay in control of approvals.
Why this matters
Every new signup shouldn’t force another full ID upload to another opaque database. Verify once, then approve specific disclosures when a service actually needs them.

3:57 PM
PM Feedback First
This modal is trying to do too much. It's supposed to answer "what just happened after I uploaded my ID?" but instead it's explaining the entire Dokimos product philosophy, technical architecture, and trust model. A user who just signed up doesn't need a 500-word essay.

The core problems:

Too long - this should be 3-4 sentences, not 8 sections
Wrong audience - mixing beginner explanations with technical details
Buried value - the good stuff (proof of age without birthdate) is hidden in section 3
Jargon-heavy - TEE, cryptographic attestation, HTTPS analogy
No clear next action - just dumps information, doesn't guide the user
What this modal should actually do: Tell the user what happened, why it's safe, and what they can do now. That's it.

Design Feedback (Specific Copy Problems)
Problem 1: The Four Steps Mix Mechanics and Benefits
Current structure:

1. You upload your ID (mechanic)
2. Secured processing (mechanic)
3. You get proof (benefit)
4. Share on your terms (benefit)
Why this is confusing:

Steps 1-2 explain HOW it works
Steps 3-4 explain WHY it matters
User doesn't know if this is a timeline or a feature list
Better approach: Either make it all timeline (what happened step-by-step) OR all benefits (what this means for you). Don't mix.

Problem 2: Technical Jargon Throughout
Jargon examples:

"Trusted execution environment (TEE)" - acronym in parentheses doesn't help
"Cryptographic attestation" - what does this mean to a regular user?
"Like HTTPS" - assumes they know what HTTPS is
"Attestations can be published" - passive, abstract
Each of these needs plain language replacement.

Problem 3: Good Ideas Buried in Bad Sections
You identified the good parts:

✅ "Proof of age without exposing your full birthdate"
✅ "You receive a credential you can use to approve requests"
✅ "Encrypted connection"
✅ "Not a stored copy of your ID for casual browsing"
These are scattered across 4 different sections. They should be front and center.

Problem 4: "What Happens to Your ID?" Is Technical
Current:

Your ID is sent encrypted (like HTTPS) to a secure environment 
that can verify it and produce a signed result. The goal is 
verification — not keeping your document as an ordinary file 
for employees to open.
Problems:

"like HTTPS" assumes technical knowledge
"produce a signed result" is jargon
"not keeping your document as an ordinary file for employees to open" is the right message but awkwardly phrased
Better:

Your ID is checked in a secure environment where no one, not 
even Dokimos staff, can access it. We verify the information 
and create a proof you control. We don't store your ID as a 
file anyone can open.
Problem 5: Em Dashes Everywhere
Your request: remove all em dashes.

Current examples:

"approve requests — not a stored copy"
"your identity was verified — so partners can rely"
"verification — not keeping your document"
"rather than 'trust us'"
These need to be replaced with periods (new sentences) or commas.

Problem 6: "Partners" Should Be "Organizations"
Current: "so partners can rely on the check"

Better: "so organizations can rely on the check"

You're right that "partners" is too cozy. These are businesses using your ID, not your friends.

Problem 7: "Attestations" Is Too Technical

Here’s what was going on and what we did.
### Why things “stopped”
1. **Backend** – The earlier failure was from **account typing** under strict TypeScript (implicitly has type 'any'). The project runs with **tsx src/index.ts**, not ts-node, but the same typing issue would still bite if ReturnType<typeof mnemonicToAccount> didn’t resolve cleanly.
2. **Ports** – **EADDRINUSE** is why a *second* start failed:
   - **8081** – something else (usually another next dev) was already bound.
   - **8080** – same for the TEE: a **node.exe** process was already listening.
Right now on your machine **node.exe PID 9144** is listening on **8080** and **node.exe PID 7444** on **8081**. So the stack is **already up**; starting again fails until those are stopped or you use other ports.
### What we changed
account is now explicitly typed as **HDAccount** from viem (matches mnemonicToAccount’s return type):
821:825:c:\Users\pkroh\dokimos-tee\src\index.ts
  let account: HDAccount;
  try {
    account = mnemonicToAccount(mnemonic);

### Smoke checks (just run)
- **http://127.0.0.1:8081/app/vault** → **200**
- **http://127.0.0.1:8081/verifier/dashboard** → **200**
- **http://127.0.0.1:8080/** → **404 JSON** (“Route GET:/ not found”) — normal if there is no root route; the server is still answering.
### If you need a clean restart
Stop the old Node processes (e.g. Task Manager → end **9144** and **7444**, or close the terminals that started them), then run npm run dev in dokimos-tee and dokimos-app-v2 again. Until those ports are free, new instances will keep exiting with **EADDRINUSE**.

4:03 PM
PM Feedback First
You have a responsive design problem. Some screens (vault, activity, settings) are locked to mobile dimensions and don't expand on desktop. This breaks the user experience when someone opens the app on a laptop.

The issue:

Screens are built with fixed mobile widths (probably max-w-[375px] or similar)
They don't adapt to larger viewports
Content is centered but stays phone-sized even on desktop
What you need:

Responsive layouts that work on mobile (375px), tablet (768px), and desktop (1024px+)
Fluid widths that expand to use available space
Proper breakpoints using Tailwind's responsive utilities
Design Feedback (Responsive Layout Patterns)
Current problem pattern (mobile-only):

tsx
<div className="max-w-[375px] mx-auto">
  {/* Content locked to phone width */}
</div>
This works on mobile but looks tiny on desktop.

Better pattern (responsive):

tsx
<div className="max-w-[375px] md:max-w-2xl lg:max-w-4xl mx-auto px-4">
  {/* Grows from mobile → tablet → desktop */}
</div>
Breakpoints:

max-w-[375px] on mobile (default, no prefix)
md:max-w-2xl on tablet (768px and up)
lg:max-w-4xl on desktop (1024px and up)
Complete Cursor Prompt (Fix Responsive Layout)
Fix responsive layout issues in user-facing screens. Currently vault, activity, and settings are locked to mobile width and don't expand on desktop.

PROBLEM:
Screens use fixed mobile widths (max-w-[375px] or similar) and don't adapt to larger viewports. Content stays phone-sized even on laptop screens.

SOLUTION:
Make layouts responsive using Tailwind breakpoints so they expand appropriately on tablet and desktop.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
STEP 1: FIND THE AFFECTED SCREENS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Search for fixed-width layouts:
```bash
# Find files with mobile-only max-width
grep -r "max-w-\[375px\]\|max-w-\[414px\]\|max-w-sm" dokimos-app-v2/src/app --include="*.tsx" -l

# Check vault, activity, settings specifically
grep "max-w" dokimos-app-v2/src/app/vault/page.tsx
grep "max-w" dokimos-app-v2/src/app/activity/page.tsx  
grep "max-w" dokimos-app-v2/src/app/settings/page.tsx
```

PASTE OUTPUT to see which files need fixing.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
STEP 2: APPLY RESPONSIVE LAYOUT PATTERN
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

For each affected screen, replace mobile-only widths with responsive breakpoints.

FIND THIS PATTERN:
```tsx
<div className="max-w-[375px] mx-auto">
  {/* content */}
</div>
```

REPLACE WITH:
```tsx
<div className="w-full max-w-[375px] md:max-w-2xl lg:max-w-4xl mx-auto px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8">
  {/* content */}
</div>
```

EXPLANATION OF CLASSES:
- w-full: Take full width of parent
- max-w-[375px]: Mobile default (375px)
- md:max-w-2xl: Tablet (672px at 768px breakpoint)
- lg:max-w-4xl: Desktop (896px at 1024px breakpoint)
- mx-auto: Center horizontally
- px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8: Responsive padding

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
STEP 3: FIX SPECIFIC SCREENS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

VAULT SCREEN (dokimos-app-v2/src/app/vault/page.tsx):

Find the main container (usually the outermost div after the component declaration).

BEFORE:
```tsx
export default function VaultPage() {
  return (
    <div className="min-h-screen bg-slate-50">
      <div className="max-w-[375px] mx-auto">
        {/* vault content */}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}
```

AFTER:
```tsx
export default function VaultPage() {
  return (
    <div className="min-h-screen bg-slate-50">
      <div className="w-full max-w-[375px] md:max-w-2xl lg:max-w-4xl mx-auto px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8 py-6">
        {/* vault content */}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}
```

<div className="w-full max-w-[375px] md:max-w-2xl lg:max-w-4xl mx-auto px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8 py-6">

<div className="w-full max-w-[375px] md:max-w-2xl lg:max-w-4xl mx-auto px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8 py-6">

</div>
```

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
STEP 4: FIX CARDS AND COMPONENTS INSIDE
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Some screens might have individual cards or sections with fixed widths.

FIND PATTERNS LIKE:
```tsx
<div className="w-[343px] mx-auto">  {/* Fixed card width */}
<div className="max-w-sm">           {/* Small max-width */}
```

REPLACE WITH:
```tsx
<div className="w-full">  {/* Let it fill the responsive container */}
```

The parent container already handles max-width, so child elements should be w-full.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
STEP 6: ADDITIONAL RESPONSIVE PATTERNS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

If screens have grids or multi-column layouts:

MOBILE-ONLY GRID:
```tsx
<div className="grid grid-cols-1 gap-4">
```

RESPONSIVE GRID:
```tsx
<div className="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-4">
```

FONT SIZES:
```tsx
{/* Mobile to desktop font scaling */}
<h1 className="text-2xl md:text-3xl lg:text-4xl">
<p className="text-sm md:text-base">
```

SPACING:
```tsx
{/* Responsive spacing */}
<div className="space-y-4 md:space-y-6 lg:space-y-8">
<div className="p-4 md:p-6 lg:p-8">
```

Quick Reference (Responsive Width Classes)
tsx
{/* Basic responsive container */}
<div className="w-full max-w-[375px] md:max-w-2xl lg:max-w-4xl mx-auto px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8">

{/* What this means: */}
// Mobile (default):     375px wide, 16px padding
// Tablet (768px+):      672px wide, 24px padding  
// Desktop (1024px+):    896px wide, 32px padding
// All sizes:            Centered horizontally

sm: 640px
md: 768px
lg: 1024px
xl: 1280px
2xl: 1536px

return <VerifierDashboard publicView />;
/verifier/dashboard:

tsx
return <VerifierDashboard />;
Same component, different prop. The publicView={true} flag likely:

Shows demo data instead of real API data
Skips authentication checks
Hides logout/session UI
Maybe changes some text ("demo" vs "real")
So the question is: Do you actually NEED two separate URLs for this?

Design Feedback (Two Scenarios)
Scenario A: You Need Both URLs
Use case:

/business = public demo for prospects ("see what the dashboard looks like")
/verifier/dashboard = real authenticated dashboard for actual verifiers
Examples where this makes sense:

Stripe has demo mode vs. live mode
Linear has public project boards vs. authenticated workspaces
You want to show the UI without requiring signup
Keep both routes.

Scenario B: You Don't Need Public Demo Mode
Use case:

You only have one dashboard: the authenticated verifier dashboard
The publicView mode was a quick hack for testing
There's no product reason for a "try before you buy" demo
Consolidate to one route:

Delete /business route
Keep only /verifier/dashboard (authenticated)
Remove publicView prop entirely from VerifierDashboard
The Key Question
Why does /business exist with publicView={true}?

Looking back at your context, you said:

"I'm debating removing the complete login screen for the Verifier dashboard because it's causing unnecessary authentication errors, and I'm not sure if it actually adds anything valuable to the demo."

And I said:

"Kill the login page. For the demo, just load the dashboard directly with 'Viewing as: Airbnb' in the header."

So /business with publicView was created as a workaround to show the dashboard without authentication for the demo.

My Recommendation
You have THREE options:

Option 1: Keep Both (Two Audiences)
Routes:

/business — public demo (no login required)
/verifier/dashboard — authenticated verifier dashboard
Use case:

Marketing/sales wants a live demo link
You show /business in your demo to Gaj/Soubhik
Real verifiers use /verifier/dashboard with login
Pro: Clear separation of demo vs. real Con: Maintaining two code paths (publicView logic)

Option 2: Consolidate to /business Only (Demo-First)
Delete:

/verifier/dashboard
/verifier/login
/verifier/signup
All auth logic
Keep:

/business as the only dashboard
Hardcoded "Viewing as: Airbnb"
Demo data only
Use case:

This is a POC/demo project for the fellowship
You're not building a real multi-tenant SaaS
No actual verifiers will use this
Pro: Simplest, no auth complexity Con: Can't show "real" login flow if asked

Option 3: Consolidate to /verifier/dashboard Only (Production-Like)
Delete:

/business route
publicView prop from VerifierDashboard
Keep:

/verifier/dashboard (authenticated)
/verifier/login (but load dashboard directly with demo session)
Mock session instead of real auth
Implementation:

tsx
// In /verifier/dashboard/page.tsx
export default function VerifierDashboardPage() {
  // Hardcode demo session
  const mockSession = {
    company: 'Airbnb',
    email: 'verify@airbnb.com'
  };
  
  return <VerifierDashboard session={mockSession} />;
}
Use case:

You want the URL structure to look "real" (/verifier/dashboard)
But skip actual authentication for the demo
Shows product thinking (proper route naming)
Pro: Production-like structure, no auth headaches Con: /verifier/login becomes pointless (redirect to dashboard immediately)

What I Think You Should Do
Based on your fellowship context:

You said the demo is this week and the evaluation is on "demo quality and narrative." You're showing Gaj (product) and Soubhik (cryptoeconomics).

Go with Option 2: Consolidate to /business only.

Why:

Simpler: No auth logic to debug or explain
Clearer demo narrative: "This is what Airbnb sees" (one URL, one story)
No confusion: You won't accidentally show login screens or auth errors
Saves time: Delete /verifier/*, focus on making /business perfect
The cleanup:

bash
# Delete verifier routes
rm -rf dokimos-app-v2/src/app/verifier

# Remove publicView prop (no longer needed)
# In VerifierDashboard component, delete all `if (publicView)` logic
# Just render demo data always

# Update any links in the app
# Change /verifier/dashboard → /business
After demo, if you need real auth, you can rebuild it.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
STEP 1: FIX MAIN CONTAINER WIDTH
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

File: dokimos-app-v2/src/components/verifier/VerifierDashboard.tsx

FIND the main container div (usually the outermost div in the return statement).

It probably looks like:
```tsx
<div className="min-h-screen bg-slate-50">
  <header>...</header>
  <main className="max-w-[375px] mx-auto p-4">
    {/* content */}
  </main>
</div>
```

OR:
```tsx
<div className="min-h-screen bg-slate-50">
  <div className="max-w-sm mx-auto">
    {/* content */}
  </div>
</div>
```

REPLACE the main content container with responsive width:
```tsx
<div className="min-h-screen bg-slate-50">
  <header className="bg-white border-b border-slate-200">
    <div className="max-w-7xl mx-auto px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8 py-4">
      {/* header content */}
    </div>
  </header>
  
  <main className="max-w-7xl mx-auto px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8 py-6 md:py-8">
    {/* dashboard content */}
  </main>
</div>
```

EXPLANATION:
- max-w-7xl: Large desktop max-width (1280px)
- mx-auto: Center horizontally
- px-4 md:px-6 lg:px-8: Responsive horizontal padding
- py-6 md:py-8: Responsive vertical padding
