}
What they verify:

The face match result is included in the signed message, so:

If signature is valid
And message includes bio:{"faceMatch":true,"confidence":0.98}
Then the TEE cryptographically attests the face matched
What this proves:

Face verification happened in the TEE
Not just in the browser (can't be bypassed)
Result is cryptographically signed
What Airbnb sees in demo:

✓ Face matched ID photo (98% confidence)
  Face Match: true
  Confidence: 98%
  Liveness Detected: true
  Verified in TEE: ✓

The Complete Verification Flow (What Soubhik Wants to See)
1. Airbnb receives attestation from Jordan
        ↓
2. Extract signature, message, signer
        ↓
3. Verify signature cryptographically
   → Proves: Message not tampered, signed by TEE
        ↓
4. Check TEE metadata
   → Open EigenCloud dashboard
   → Verify: Intel TDX, production mode, quote valid
   → Proves: Ran in real TEE hardware
        ↓
5. Check source code
   → Go to GitHub commit
   → Review code in dokimos-tee/src/
   → Proves: Can see what the TEE does
        ↓
6. Verify build provenance
   → EigenCloud shows: commit SHA = image hash
   → Proves: GitHub code = running code
        ↓
7. Check biometric verification
   → Face match result in signed message
   → Proves: TEE verified the face, not browser
        ↓
8. Conclusion: All checks pass
   → Airbnb trusts the verification
   → No trust in Dokimos needed
   → Pure cryptographic proof

Key Technical Points for Soubhik
1. No Trust in Dokimos
"Airbnb doesn't trust our database. They don't trust our API.
They verify cryptographic proof independently. If we went rogue
tomorrow, their verification would fail."

2. Hardware Root of Trust
"The trust anchor is Intel TDX hardware. Intel signs the quote.
We can't fake that. The TEE proves what code ran on what hardware."

3. Reproducible Builds
"The build is deterministic. Anyone can rebuild from source
and verify the hash matches. No proprietary secrets."

4. Complete Auditability
"Every verification is logged with timestamp and signature.
Immutable audit trail. Compliance-ready."

5. Biometric Verification in TEE
"Face matching happens in the TEE, not the browser. The
attestation proves the face matched. You can't bypass it
by manipulating client-side code."

Questions Soubhik Might Ask
Q: "What prevents you from running this in debug mode and faking the attestation?"
A: "The TEE metadata includes debugMode: false. Intel TDX hardware attests to this. If we ran in debug mode, the quote signature from Intel would reflect that, and verifiers would see it. We can't fake the Intel signature."

Q: "How do I know the GitHub code is actually what's running?"
A: "EigenCloud provides build provenance. The image hash is deterministic and matches the commit SHA. You can rebuild from source yourself and verify the hash matches. If it doesn't, the verification fails."

Q: "What if you modify the code after deployment?"
A: "You can't modify code in a running TEE — that's the point of sealed execution. If we wanted to change the code, we'd have to:

Update GitHub
Rebuild the image
Redeploy to a new enclave
The image hash would change
Verifiers would see a different code measurement
Old attestations would still be verifiable against the old code. New attestations would reference the new code. Complete auditability."

Q: "What's your slashing mechanism if the TEE misbehaves?"
A: "That's the EigenLayer integration. Operators stake collateral. If a TEE:

Runs in debug mode when claiming production
Produces invalid attestations
Gets compromised
Slashing proofs can be submitted. The stake gets slashed. This creates economic security on top of the cryptographic security."

(Note: You might not have slashing fully implemented in the POC, but you should understand the model)

Oh, I see. Okay, so the attestation the user gets has all this information, which is exactly what has the claims they can check the signature. 9:43 AM
YES — exactly right! The attestation contains:

The claims (Full Name, Age 18+, etc.)
All the verification data (signature, TEE proof, build hash, etc.)
Let me explain how the attestation flows from user to verifier.

Attestation Routing: User → Verifier
The Complete Flow
1. User completes verification in Dokimos app
   → Gets attestation stored locally
   
2. Airbnb sends verification request via API
   → "Please verify your identity to list properties"
   
3. User sees request in Dokimos app
   → Reviews what Airbnb is asking for
   → Clicks "Approve & Share"
   
4. Dokimos app sends attestation to Dokimos backend
   → Backend routes it to Airbnb
   
5. Airbnb receives complete attestation
   → Can verify independently
Option 1: Direct API Call (Simpler)
What happens when user clicks "Approve":

typescript
// In Dokimos app (user side)
async function approveRequest(requestId: string) {
  // Get the attestation from local storage or context
  const attestation = getStoredAttestation();
  
  // Send to Dokimos backend
  const response = await fetch('/api/approve-request', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      requestId: requestId,
      attestation: attestation  // Full attestation object
    })
  });
  
  return response.json();
}
Dokimos backend receives the approval:

typescript
// In Dokimos backend (dokimos-tee or separate API server)
server.post('/api/approve-request', async (request, reply) => {
  const { requestId, attestation } = request.body;
  
  // 1. Look up the verification request
  const verificationRequest = await db.requests.findById(requestId);
  
  if (!verificationRequest) {
    return reply.code(404).send({ error: 'Request not found' });
  }
  
  // 2. Extract verifier's callback info
  const verifier = verificationRequest.verifierId; // 'airbnb'
  const verifierWebhook = verificationRequest.webhookUrl; // 'https://api.airbnb.com/dokimos-webhook'
  
  // 3. Forward attestation to verifier
  const airbnbResponse = await fetch(verifierWebhook, {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
      'Content-Type': 'application/json',
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${DOKIMOS_SECRET_KEY}` // Proves it's from Dokimos
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      requestId: requestId,
      userId: verificationRequest.userId,
      status: 'approved',
      attestation: attestation,  // Full attestation with all verification data
      approvedAt: new Date().toISOString()
    })
  });
  
  // 4. Update request status
  await db.requests.update(requestId, {
    status: 'approved',
    attestation: attestation,
    approvedAt: new Date().toISOString()
  });
  
  return { success: true };
});
Airbnb receives the attestation:

typescript
// In Airbnb's backend
app.post('/dokimos-webhook', async (req, res) => {
  const { requestId, userId, status, attestation, approvedAt } = req.body;
  
  // Verify webhook is actually from Dokimos (check signature)
  const isValidWebhook = verifyWebhookSignature(req);
  if (!isValidWebhook) {
    return res.status(401).send({ error: 'Invalid webhook signature' });
  }
  
  if (status === 'approved') {
    // Now Airbnb has the FULL attestation
    console.log('Received attestation:', attestation);
    
    /*
    attestation = {
      attributes: {
        fullName: "Jordan Lee",
        ageOver18: true,
        documentNotExpired: true
      },
      biometricVerification: {
        faceMatch: true,
        confidence: 0.98,
        livenessDetected: true,
        verifiedAt: "2026-04-05T14:34:22Z"
      },
      timestamp: "2026-04-05T14:34:22Z",
      message: "IdentityAttestation|{...}|2026-04-05T14:34:22Z|bio:{...}",
      messageHash: "0x3a8f1b2c...",
      signature: "0x8f3c92a1b4d5e6f7...",
      signer: "0x742d35E9c07fF4c8...",
      tee: {
        platform: "INTEL_TDX",
        enclaveId: "a7f3c89d2e1b...",
        debugMode: false,
        quoteSignature: "0x4b8c3a..."
      },
      eigen: {
        appId: "0x00658e70d8880910277592b3b41f9dd3fe4ce5fd",
        sourceRepo: "https://github.com/pranavirohit/dokimos",
        commitSha: "a3f892b4c1d5e2f3...",
        imageDigest: "sha256:8f3c92a1b4d5e6f7..."
      }
    }
    */
    
    // Airbnb can now verify this attestation independently
    const isValid = await verifyAttestation(attestation);
    
    if (isValid) {
      // Update user in Airbnb database
      await db.users.update(userId, {
        identityVerified: true,
        verificationAttestation: attestation,
        verifiedAt: approvedAt
      });
      
      // Enable host features
      await enableHostFeatures(userId);
      
      // Send confirmation email
      await sendEmail(userId, 'You can now list properties!');
    } else {
      // Attestation invalid - reject
      console.error('Invalid attestation received');
    }
  }
  
  // Acknowledge receipt
  res.json({ received: true });
});
Option 2: Polling (Alternative)
If Airbnb doesn't want to expose a webhook:

typescript
// Airbnb polls Dokimos API for status updates
async function checkVerificationStatus(requestId: string) {
  const response = await fetch(`https://api.dokimos.app/requests/${requestId}`, {
    headers: {
      'Authorization': `Bearer ${AIRBNB_API_KEY}`
    }
  });
  
  const request = await response.json();
  
  /*
  request = {
    id: "req_abc123",
    status: "approved",
    userId: "jordan@email.com",
    attestation: { ... full attestation ... },
    approvedAt: "2026-04-05T14:34:22Z"
  }
  */
  
  if (request.status === 'approved') {
    // Process the attestation
    await verifyAndStoreAttestation(request.attestation);
  }
}

// Poll every 5 seconds until approved or timeout
const pollInterval = setInterval(async () => {
  const request = await checkVerificationStatus(requestId);
  
  if (request.status === 'approved' || request.status === 'denied') {
    clearInterval(pollInterval);
  }
}, 5000);
What's in the Attestation Object
The complete attestation that gets sent to Airbnb:

typescript
interface Attestation {
  // The verified claims (what Airbnb requested)
  attributes: {
    fullName?: string;
    ageOver18?: boolean;
    ageOver21?: boolean;
    documentNotExpired?: boolean;
    nationality?: string;
    dateOfBirth?: string;
    address?: string;
  };
  
  // Biometric verification (from TEE)
  biometricVerification?: {
    faceMatch: boolean;
    confidence: number;
    livenessDetected: boolean;
    verifiedAt: string;
    error?: string;
  };
  
  // Timestamp
  timestamp: string;
  
  // The signed message
  message: string;  // "IdentityAttestation|{attributes}|timestamp|bio:{biometric}"
  messageHash: string;  // Hash of the message
  
  // Cryptographic signature
  signature: string;  // "0x8f3c92a1b4d5e6f7..."
  signer: string;     // "0x742d35E9c07fF4c8..." (TEE's Ethereum address)
  
  // TEE metadata (for hardware verification)
  tee: {
    platform: string;        // "INTEL_TDX"
    enclaveId: string;       // Unique enclave identifier
    debugMode: boolean;      // false = production
    quoteSignature: string;  // Intel's signature on the quote
    tcbInfo?: {
      version: string;
      level: string;  // "UpToDate"
    };
  };
  
  // EigenCloud metadata (for source verification)
  eigen: {
    appId: string;           // "0x00658e70d8880910277592b3b41f9dd3fe4ce5fd"
    sourceRepo: string;      // "https://github.com/pranavirohit/dokimos"
    commitSha: string;       // "a3f892b4c1d5e2f3..."
    imageDigest: string;     // "sha256:8f3c92a1b4d5e6f7..."
  };
}
This single object contains EVERYTHING Airbnb needs to:

See the verified claims
Verify the signature
Check the TEE hardware
Audit the source code
Verify the build provenance
Confirm biometric verification
Database Schema (Backend Storage)
Verification Requests Table:

typescript
interface VerificationRequest {
  id: string;                    // "req_abc123"
  verifierId: string;            // "airbnb"
  verifierName: string;          // "Airbnb"
  webhookUrl?: string;           // "https://api.airbnb.com/dokimos-webhook"
  
  userId: string;                // "jordan@email.com"
  workflowId: string;            // "workflow_host_verification"
  
  attributesRequested: string[]; // ["fullName", "ageOver18", "documentNotExpired"]
  
  status: 'pending' | 'approved' | 'denied' | 'expired';
  
  requestedAt: string;           // ISO timestamp
  respondedAt?: string;          // When user approved/denied
  
  attestation?: Attestation;     // Full attestation object (stored when approved)
}
When user approves:

typescript
// Update request
await db.requests.update(requestId, {
  status: 'approved',
  respondedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
  attestation: attestation  // Store the full attestation
});

// Forward to verifier
await notifyVerifier(request, attestation);
How Airbnb Looks Up Attestations
Option 1: Query by Request ID

typescript
// Airbnb knows the requestId from when they created the request
const response = await fetch(`https://api.dokimos.app/requests/${requestId}`, {
  headers: { 'Authorization': `Bearer ${AIRBNB_API_KEY}` }
});

const request = await response.json();

if (request.status === 'approved') {
  const attestation = request.attestation;
  // Now Airbnb has the full attestation
}
Option 2: Query by User ID

typescript
// Get all attestations for a specific user
const response = await fetch(`https://api.dokimos.app/attestations/user/${userId}`, {
  headers: { 'Authorization': `Bearer ${AIRBNB_API_KEY}` }
});

const attestations = await response.json();

// Find the most recent one for the "Host Verification" workflow
const hostAttestation = attestations.find(a => 
  a.workflow === 'Host Verification' && 
  a.status === 'approved'
);
Option 3: Webhook Push (Recommended)

Airbnb doesn't have to poll — Dokimos pushes the attestation when approved:

typescript
// Airbnb just provides webhook URL when creating workflow
const workflow = {
  name: "Host Verification",
  attributesRequired: ["fullName", "ageOver18", "documentNotExpired"],
  webhookUrl: "https://api.airbnb.com/dokimos-webhook"
};

// When user approves, Dokimos automatically POSTs to that webhook
// Airbnb receives the attestation without polling
Security Considerations
1. Webhook Authentication
When Dokimos sends attestation to Airbnb's webhook:

typescript
// Dokimos signs the webhook payload
const payload = JSON.stringify({
  requestId,
  userId,
  status: 'approved',
  attestation,
  approvedAt
});

const webhookSignature = await signMessage({
  message: payload,
  privateKey: DOKIMOS_WEBHOOK_SIGNING_KEY
});

// Send to Airbnb
await fetch(airbnbWebhook, {
  method: 'POST',
  headers: {
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',
    'X-Dokimos-Signature': webhookSignature,
    'X-Dokimos-Timestamp': timestamp
  },
  body: payload
});
Airbnb verifies the webhook:

typescript
// Airbnb's webhook handler
app.post('/dokimos-webhook', async (req, res) => {
  const signature = req.headers['x-dokimos-signature'];
  const timestamp = req.headers['x-dokimos-timestamp'];
  const payload = JSON.stringify(req.body);
  
  // Verify signature
  const isValid = await verifyWebhookSignature(
    payload,
    signature,
    DOKIMOS_PUBLIC_KEY
  );
  
  if (!isValid) {
    return res.status(401).send({ error: 'Invalid signature' });
  }
  
  // Verify timestamp (prevent replay attacks)
  const age = Date.now() - new Date(timestamp).getTime();
  if (age > 5 * 60 * 1000) { // 5 minutes
    return res.status(401).send({ error: 'Webhook too old' });
  }
  
  // Process attestation
  // ...
});
2. API Authentication
When Airbnb polls for attestation status:

typescript
// Airbnb provides API key
const response = await fetch(`https://api.dokimos.app/requests/${requestId}`, {
  headers: {
    'Authorization': `Bearer ${AIRBNB_API_KEY}`
  }
});

// Dokimos verifies API key
server.get('/requests/:id', async (request, reply) => {
  const apiKey = request.headers.authorization?.replace('Bearer ', '');
  
  // Verify API key
  const verifier = await db.verifiers.findByApiKey(apiKey);
  if (!verifier) {
    return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid API key' });
  }
  
  // Check that this verifier owns this request
  const req = await db.requests.findById(request.params.id);
  if (req.verifierId !== verifier.id) {
    return reply.code(403).send({ error: 'Access denied' });
  }
  
  // Return request with attestation
  return req;
});

Key Takeaway
The attestation is self-contained.

Airbnb receives ONE object that contains:

The claims (what they asked for)
The cryptographic proof (signature)
The hardware proof (TEE metadata)
The source code proof (commit SHA, image hash)
The biometric proof (face match result)
They don't need to make 5 separate API calls to verify each piece. It's all in the attestation.

This is why the trust model works — verifiers can verify independently without relying on Dokimos's infrastructure.

Does this clarify the routing? User approves → Dokimos backend receives → Forwards to Airbnb → Airbnb verifies independently.

Am I missing anything else? The demo is done right after I showed a walkthrough for how the person can verify the information, right?

Prep answers:

Slashing: Operators stake ETH; false attestations → stake slashed via EigenLayer
Collusion: Multiple attestations from different TEE instances; threshold signatures
Intel vulnerability: Security patches → new code measurement; old attestations still verifiable against old code

## Phase 2: Implementation

Based on the assessment, implement missing components.

### If Backend is Missing:

#### Step 1: Add Encryption Functions

**File:** `dokimos-tee/src/idStorage.ts` (create new file)
```typescript
import crypto from 'crypto';

// In-memory storage for encrypted IDs (production would use encrypted database)
const encryptedIds = new Map();

// Derive encryption key from userId
export function deriveUserKey(userId: string): Buffer {
  const salt = 'dokimos-salt-v1'; // In production, use random salt per user
  return crypto.pbkdf2Sync(userId, salt, 100000, 32, 'sha256');
}

// Encrypt ID image
export function encryptIdImage(imageBase64: string, userId: string): string {
  const key = deriveUserKey(userId);
  const iv = crypto.randomBytes(16);
  
  const cipher = crypto.createCipheriv('aes-256-gcm', key, iv);
  
  let encrypted = cipher.update(imageBase64, 'utf8', 'hex');
  encrypted += cipher.final('hex');
  
  const authTag = cipher.getAuthTag();
  
  // Combine iv + authTag + encrypted data
  return iv.toString('hex') + ':' + authTag.toString('hex') + ':' + encrypted;
}

// Decrypt ID image
export function decryptIdImage(encryptedData: string, userId: string): string | null {
  try {
    const key = deriveUserKey(userId);
    const parts = encryptedData.split(':');
    
    if (parts.length !== 3) {
      throw new Error('Invalid encrypted data format');
    }
    
    const iv = Buffer.from(parts[0], 'hex');
    const authTag = Buffer.from(parts[1], 'hex');
    const encrypted = parts[2];
    
    const decipher = crypto.createDecipheriv('aes-256-gcm', key, iv);
    decipher.setAuthTag(authTag);
    
    let decrypted = decipher.update(encrypted, 'hex', 'utf8');
    decrypted += decipher.final('utf8');
    
    return decrypted;
  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Decryption failed:', error);
    return null;
  }
}

// Store encrypted ID
export function storeEncryptedId(userId: string, encryptedData: string): void {
  encryptedIds.set(userId, encryptedData);
}

// Retrieve encrypted ID
export function getEncryptedId(userId: string): string | null {
  return encryptedIds.get(userId) || null;
}
```

#### Step 2: Update /verify Endpoint to Store Encrypted ID

**File:** `dokimos-tee/src/index.ts`
```typescript
import {
  encryptIdImage,
  storeEncryptedId
} from './idStorage';

server.post('/verify', async (request, reply) => {
  try {
    const { imageBase64, livePhotoBase64, userId } = request.body;

    // Existing verification logic...
    const extractedAttributes = await extractAttributesFromDocument(imageBase64);
    
    let faceVerification = null;
    if (livePhotoBase64) {
      const idPhoto = await extractIdPhoto(imageBase64);
      if (idPhoto) {
        faceVerification = await compareFaces(idPhoto, livePhotoBase64);
      }
    }

    // NEW: Encrypt and store the ID image
    if (userId && imageBase64) {
      const encryptedId = encryptIdImage(imageBase64, userId);
      storeEncryptedId(userId, encryptedId);
      console.log(`✓ Encrypted ID stored for user: ${userId}`);
    }

    // Build attestation response...
    const response = buildSignedAttestationResponse(
      extractedAttributes,
      faceVerification
    );

    return response;
  } catch (error) {
    fastify.log.error(error);
    return reply.code(500).send({ error: 'Verification failed' });
  }
});
```

#### Step 3: Add /re-verify Endpoint

**File:** `dokimos-tee/src/index.ts`
```typescript
import {
  getEncryptedId,
  decryptIdImage
} from './idStorage';

server.post('/re-verify', async (request, reply) => {
  try {
    const { userId } = request.body;

    if (!userId) {
      return reply.code(400).send({ error: 'userId required' });
    }

    // Retrieve encrypted ID
    const encryptedId = getEncryptedId(userId);
    
    if (!encryptedId) {
      return reply.code(404).send({ error: 'No encrypted ID found for this user' });
    }

    // Decrypt the ID
    const imageBase64 = decryptIdImage(encryptedId, userId);
    
    if (!imageBase64) {
      return reply.code(500).send({ error: 'Failed to decrypt ID' });
    }

    console.log(`✓ Re-verifying user: ${userId}`);

    // Re-extract attributes from the stored ID
    const extractedAttributes = await extractAttributesFromDocument(imageBase64);

    // No live photo for re-verification (could add liveness if needed)
    const faceVerification = null;

    // Create fresh attestation with new timestamp
    const timestamp = new Date().toISOString();
    const message = `IdentityAttestation|${JSON.stringify(extractedAttributes)}|${timestamp}`;
    const messageHash = hashMessage(message);
    const signature = await account.signMessage({ message });

    const response = {
      attributes: extractedAttributes,
      timestamp,
      message,
      messageHash,
      signature,
      signer: account.address,
      tee: generateMockTEEData(),
      eigen: generateMockEigenData(),
      reverified: true  // Flag to indicate this was a re-verification
    };

    return response;
  } catch (error) {
    fastify.log.error(error);
    return reply.code(500).send({ error: 'Re-verification failed' });
  }
});
```

---

### If Frontend is Missing:

#### Step 1: Update Vault to Show Re-verify Button

**File:** `dokimos-app-v2/src/components/DokimosFlow.tsx`

Find the `Screen03Vault` component and add:
```typescript
function Screen03Vault() {
  const { attestationData } = useDokimosContext();
  const [hasEncryptedId, setHasEncryptedId] = useState(false);
  const [isReverifying, setIsReverifying] = useState(false);

  useEffect(() => {
    // Check if user has encrypted ID stored
    const encrypted = localStorage.getItem('dokimos_has_encrypted_id');
    setHasEncryptedId(encrypted === 'true');
  }, []);

  async function handleReVerify() {
    try {
      setIsReverifying(true);

      const response = await fetch('/api/re-verify', {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify({
          userId: session?.user?.email
        })
      });

      const data = await response.json();

      // Update attestation data
      setAttestationData(data);
      
      // Store new attestation
      localStorage.setItem('dokimos_attestation_data', JSON.stringify(data));

      alert('Re-verification successful! New attestation created.');
    } catch (error) {
      console.error('Re-verification failed:', error);
      alert('Re-verification failed. Please try again.');
    } finally {
      setIsReverifying(false);
    }
  }

  return (
    
      Your Vault
      
      {/* Existing verified attributes display */}
      
        {/* ... existing code ... */}
      

      {/* NEW: Re-verify button */}
      {hasEncryptedId && (
        
          
            Need to refresh your verification? You can re-verify without 
            uploading your ID again.
          
          
            {isReverifying ? 'Re-verifying...' : 'Re-verify'}
          
        
      )}
    
  );
}
```

#### Step 2: Set localStorage Flag After First Verification

**File:** `dokimos-app-v2/src/components/DokimosFlow.tsx`

In the `Screen02VerifyProcessing` component, after successful verification:
```typescript
async function handleVerify() {
  try {
    setIsVerifying(true);

    const livePhoto = localStorage.getItem('dokimos_live_photo');

    const response = await fetch('/api/verify', {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({
        imageBase64: selectedImage,
        livePhotoBase64: livePhoto,
        userId: session?.user?.email
      })
    });

    const data = await response.json();
    
    setAttestationData(data);
    
    // NEW: Set flag that encrypted ID is stored
    localStorage.setItem('dokimos_has_encrypted_id', 'true');
    
    localStorage.removeItem('dokimos_live_photo');
    
    onNext();
  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Verification failed:', error);
  } finally {
    setIsVerifying(false);
  }
}
```

#### Step 3: Create API Proxy Route

**File:** Create `dokimos-app-v2/src/app/api/re-verify/route.ts`
```typescript
import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server';
import { getServerSession } from 'next-auth';
import { authOptions } from '../auth/[...nextauth]/route';

const TEE_ENDPOINT = process.env.TEE_ENDPOINT || 'http://localhost:8080';

export async function POST(req: NextRequest) {
  try {
    const body = await req.json();
    const session = await getServerSession(authOptions);

    const response = await fetch(`${TEE_ENDPOINT}/re-verify`, {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({
        userId: session?.user?.email || body.userId
      }),
      signal: AbortSignal.timeout(30000) // 30 second timeout
    });

    if (!response.ok) {
      const error = await response.json();
      return NextResponse.json(
        { error: error.error || 'Re-verification failed' },
        { status: response.status }
      );
    }

    const data = await response.json();
    return NextResponse.json(data);
  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Re-verify API error:', error);
    return NextResponse.json(
      { error: 'Re-verification failed' },
      { status: 500 }
    );
  }
}
```

## Phase 3: Testing

After implementation, test the complete flow:

### Backend Test
```bash
# Terminal 1: Start backend
cd dokimos-tee
npm run dev

# Terminal 2: Test re-verify endpoint
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/re-verify \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"userId":"test@example.com"}'
```

**Expected response:**
- First call: 404 (no encrypted ID yet)
- After running `/verify` with same userId: 200 with new attestation
- Check logs for "✓ Encrypted ID stored for user: test@example.com"

### Frontend Test
```bash
# Start frontend
cd dokimos-app-v2
npm run dev
```

**Test flow:**
1. Complete first-time verification
2. Check localStorage: `dokimos_has_encrypted_id` should be 'true'
3. Refresh page, go to vault
4. "Re-verify" button should appear
5. Click "Re-verify"
6. New attestation should appear with updated timestamp
7. Check network tab: POST to `/api/re-verify` should succeed

---

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Backend stores encrypted ID after `/verify`
- [ ] Backend `/re-verify` endpoint retrieves and decrypts ID
- [ ] Backend creates fresh attestation with new timestamp
- [ ] Frontend sets localStorage flag after first verification
- [ ] Frontend shows "Re-verify" button in vault
- [ ] Frontend calls `/api/re-verify` successfully
- [ ] New attestation displayed with `reverified: true` flag
- [ ] No errors in console or backend logs

---

## Notes

- Encrypted ID storage is in-memory for POC (lost on server restart)
- Production would use encrypted database (e.g., PostgreSQL with pgcrypto)
- Re-verification does NOT require live photo (no face match)
- Could add optional liveness check for re-verification if needed
- Consider adding expiry timestamp to trigger re-verification prompts

## Phase 5: Issue Summary and Prioritization

After completing all checks, create a prioritized issue list:

### HIGH Priority (Must Fix Before Demo)
```markdown
1. [SECURITY] Private key hardcoded in dokimos-tee/src/index.ts line 23
   - Impact: Complete security compromise
   - Fix: Move to environment variable

2. [FUNCTIONALITY] Face verification not included in signature
   - Impact: Biometric data not cryptographically proven
   - Fix: Include bio data in signed message

3. [CRASH] Unhandled error in /verify when face models fail to load
   - Impact: Server crashes on verification
   - Fix: Wrap in try-catch, return graceful error
```

### MEDIUM Priority (Should Fix, But Not Blocking)
```markdown
1. [PERFORMANCE] Face models loaded on every request
   - Impact: Slow verification, high memory usage
   - Fix: Load models once on startup

2. [UX] No loading indicator during re-verification
   - Impact: User thinks app is frozen
   - Fix: Add loading spinner

3. [TYPE SAFETY] Using 'any' for attestation data in 5 places
   - Impact: Type errors not caught at compile time
   - Fix: Define proper interfaces
```

### LOW Priority (Nice to Have)
```markdown
1. [DOCS] Missing README in dokimos-tee
   - Impact: Hard for others to understand codebase
   - Fix: Add README with setup instructions

2. [STYLE] Inconsistent error message formatting
   - Impact: Confusing for users
   - Fix: Standardize error message format

3. [OPTIMIZATION] Uncompressed images in frontend
   - Impact: Slightly slower page load
   - Fix: Optimize images with next/image
```

import { type PrivateKeyAccount } from 'viem';

const account: PrivateKeyAccount = mnemonicToAccount(MNEMONIC);

typescript
// Change from:
localStorage.setItem('dokimos_has_encrypted_id', '1');

// To:
localStorage.setItem('dokimos_has_encrypted_id', 'true');

// And check:
const hasEncryptedId = localStorage.getItem('dokimos_has_encrypted_id') === 'true';
Priority: Low — doesn't affect functionality.

✅ What You DON'T Need to Fix
These are FINE for the demo:

✅ In-memory encrypted storage (acceptable for POC, acknowledge in demo)
✅ Attestation persistence in localStorage (works for demo)
✅ No database for verification requests (can explain via webhook/API)
✅ Type inference warnings (not user-facing)

Here's a better product description that's clearer and more compelling:

Option 1: Concise & Benefit-Focused
Your ID vault. Your control.

Verify once, share everywhere. Companies request what they need—you approve what to share. Your ID stays secure until you decide to release a verified proof.

4 verified attributes ready to share

Option 2: More Explanatory
Never upload your ID again

You verified once. Now companies can request proof of your identity—you choose what to share, when to share it. Your ID stays encrypted and secure. Only verified proofs are released.

4 verified attributes ready to share

Option 3: Action-Oriented (Recommended)
Upload once. Verify everywhere.

Companies request verification—you approve what they see. No re-uploading IDs. No waiting for manual review. Share verified proofs on your terms.

4 verified attributes ready to share

Why These Are Better:
Current version issues:

"Never upload your ID again" is good but passive
"Approve requests on your terms" is vague
"Your details stay here until you choose to release a verified proof" is wordy and unclear
Improvements:

✅ Lead with the value prop: "Upload once. Verify everywhere."
✅ Explain the flow: Request → Approve → Share proof
✅ Emphasize control: "On your terms" / "You choose"
✅ Remove jargon: "Verified proof" is clearer than technical attestation talk
✅ Active voice: "Share verified proofs" vs "details stay here"
My Recommendation:
Upload once. Verify everywhere.

Companies request verification—you approve what they see. Your ID stays secure. Only verified proofs are shared.

4 verified attributes ready to share

This version:

Starts with the tagline (memorable, clear benefit)
Explains the two-sided interaction (companies request, you approve)
Emphasizes security (ID stays secure)
Clarifies what gets shared (proofs, not raw IDs)
Ends with concrete status (4 attributes ready)
Keeps it under 30 words, scannable, and immediately understandable to non-technical users.

https://verify-sepolia.eigencloud.xyz/app/0x00658e70d8880910277592b3b41f9dd3fe4ce5fd

How can this site give you all the attestation data when multiple users are using this? How would they know what attestation data is theirs and what to look for?

12:43 PM
Great question — you've identified a key UX problem.

The Issue
That EigenCloud dashboard link shows ALL attestations for your deployed TEE app (0x00658e70...). It doesn't filter by user.

This means:

Jordan's attestation appears
Sarah's attestation appears
Michael's attestation appears
Everyone using Dokimos sees the same list
How Users Would Find Their Attestation
Users would need to:

Know their attestation ID or signature (from when they verified)
Search through the list manually
Match timestamp (e.g., "I verified on April 5 at 2:34 PM")
Match their data (e.g., look for their name in the claims)
This is obviously terrible UX for end users.

Solutions
Option 1: User-Specific Link (Best for Demo)
When the user completes verification, give them a direct link to their specific attestation:

javascript
// After verification
const attestationId = data.signature; // or unique ID

const userAttestationLink = `https://verify-sepolia.eigencloud.xyz/app/0x00658e70d8880910277592b3b41f9dd3fe4ce5fd/attestation/${attestationId}`;

// Show this link in the vault
User clicks → sees ONLY their attestation, not the full list.

Problem: EigenCloud might not support deep-linking to individual attestations (need to check their docs).

Option 2: Filter by User ID
If EigenCloud supports query parameters:

https://verify-sepolia.eigencloud.xyz/app/0x00658e70.../attestations?userId=jordan@email.com
Or filter by signature/message hash.

Problem: EigenCloud might not expose filtering API.

Option 3: Don't Send Users to EigenCloud (Recommended for POC)
For regular users: Don't send them to EigenCloud at all.

Instead, show verification details IN your app:

Your Vault
├─ Verified Attributes
├─ Verification Date
├─ Face Match: ✓
├─ [ℹ️ How does this work?] → Your explainer page
└─ [🔗 View Technical Details] → In-app verification view
Only send technical users to EigenCloud when they explicitly want to verify independently.

Option 4: Verifiers Use EigenCloud, Users Don't
This is probably the right model:

End Users (Jordan):

See verification status in Dokimos app
Can download their attestation JSON
Don't go to EigenCloud (too technical)
Verifiers (Airbnb):

Receive attestation from Jordan
Go to EigenCloud to verify it
Search by signature/hash to find the specific attestation
Verify cryptographic proof independently
Power Users (Developers, Security Auditors):

Can manually verify using the attestation data
Go to EigenCloud if they want
Use signature to find their specific attestation in the list
For Your Demo
User Side (Part 2: TEE Explainer)
Don't say:

"Technical users can click here to see their verification on EigenCloud"

Do say:

"For technical users who want to verify the cryptographic proof, they can download their attestation data and verify it independently. I'll show you what that verification looks like from the business side in a moment."

Then show the EigenCloud verification from the VERIFIER perspective in Part 4.

Verifier Side (Part 4: Verify Attestation)
When showing how Airbnb verifies Jordan's attestation:

Narration:

"Airbnb receives Jordan's attestation with her signature. They can verify this independently on the EigenCloud dashboard.

[Open EigenCloud link]

They'd search for Jordan's signature here...

[Point to the attestations list, or manually find it]

And verify: signature valid, Intel TDX hardware, source code matches, build provenance confirmed.

Airbnb doesn't trust our database—they verify the cryptographic proof themselves."

What You Should Build
In Your App (Vault Screen)
For regular users:

✓ Verified
  
Verified on: Apr 5, 2026 2:34 PM
Face matched: ✓ (98% confidence)

[ℹ️] How does this work?
Clicking info icon:

"Learn More" → /app/how-it-works (your plain language page)
"Download Attestation" → Downloads JSON file
DON'T link to EigenCloud (too confusing for regular users)
For Technical Users
If they want to verify independently, they:

Download attestation JSON from your app
Manually verify signature (using ethers.js or viem)
Optionally go to EigenCloud and search for their signature
Or you build an in-app verification UI that shows:

✓ Signature valid
✓ Hardware: Intel TDX
✓ Source: GitHub link
✓ Build verified
This is better UX than sending them to EigenCloud.

TL;DR
The EigenCloud link shows ALL attestations from your app, not user-specific ones.

Solution for demo:

Don't send regular users to EigenCloud (too technical, not user-specific)
Show verification details in your app (vault + explainer)
Use EigenCloud from the VERIFIER perspective (Part 4 of demo)
When verifiers verify attestations, they search by signature/hash to find the specific one
This matches the actual use case: businesses verify attestations, not end users.

Technical users can view verification details in their vault, but the real verification happens on the business side—I'll show you that in a moment when we look at how Airbnb verifies identities."

Then transition immediately to Part 3 (Returning User).

You STILL show EigenCloud verification, but from the verifier's perspective:

"When Airbnb receives Jordan's attestation, they verify it independently. Let me show you what that looks like.

[Open EigenCloud dashboard]

They can see all attestations from this TEE. They'd search for Jordan's signature...

[Find Jordan's attestation or point to it]

And verify: signature valid ✓, Intel TDX hardware ✓, source code on GitHub ✓, build provenance verified ✓.

Airbnb doesn't trust Dokimos—they verify the math themselves."

This makes sense because verifiers DO use EigenCloud to verify attestations.

Regular users don't verify their own attestations.

Verifiers verify attestations.

So show verification from the verifier perspective (Part 4), not the user perspective (Part 2).

This matches the actual product flow AND makes the demo clearer.

What are other important attributes we should add? One thing I'm thinking of is age over 18, and for nationality it should always be the full country name.

12:53 PM
Good thinking. Here's a comprehensive list of attributes that make sense for identity verification:

Core Identity Attributes
What You Already Have:
✅ Full Name
✅ Age Over 21
✅ Document Not Expired
What to Add:
Age Verification (Multiple Thresholds):
✅ Age Over 18 (most common threshold — voting, contracts, adult content)
✅ Age Over 21 (already have — alcohol, gambling in US)
⚠️ Age Over 13 (COPPA compliance for social media)
⚠️ Age Over 16 (GDPR consent age in some countries)
Recommendation: Add Age Over 18, keep Age Over 21. Skip 13/16 unless needed.

Nationality/Citizenship:
✅ Nationality (full country name: "United States" not "US")
Format as: ISO country name standard
Examples: "United States", "United Kingdom", "Canada", "India"
Document Information:
✅ Document Not Expired (already have)
✅ Document Type (Driver's License, Passport, National ID)
⚠️ Document Expiry Date (full date, not just "not expired")
⚠️ Document Issue Date
⚠️ Document Number (redacted or full — privacy concern)
Recommendation: Add Document Type. Add Document Expiry Date if verifiers need it for compliance.

Personal Information:
✅ Date of Birth (full date vs just age threshold)
⚠️ Gender (if on ID and relevant for some use cases)
⚠️ Address (from ID — residential verification)
⚠️ State/Province (without full address)
⚠️ Country of Residence (different from nationality)
Recommendation: Add Date of Birth (more flexible than age thresholds). Add Address if needed for KYC.

Recommended Attribute Set
Tier 1: Essential (Always Extract)
Full Name ✅
Date of Birth (NEW)
Nationality (full country name) ✅
Document Type (NEW — Driver's License, Passport, etc.)
Document Not Expired ✅
Document Expiry Date (NEW)
Tier 2: Age Thresholds (Derived from DOB)
Age Over 18 (NEW)
Age Over 21 ✅
Tier 3: Additional (Optional)
Address (if on ID and needed)
Gender (if on ID and needed)
State/Province
Implementation Changes
Backend: Update Attribute Extraction
File: dokimos-tee/src/index.ts (or wherever OCR parsing happens)

typescript
interface ExtractedAttributes {
  // Tier 1: Essential
  fullName?: string;
  dateOfBirth?: string;           // NEW: "1995-03-15" (ISO format)
  nationality?: string;            // NEW: "United States" (full country name)
  documentType?: string;           // NEW: "Driver's License" | "Passport" | "National ID"
  documentNotExpired?: boolean;
  documentExpiryDate?: string;     // NEW: "2028-12-31" (ISO format)
  
  // Tier 2: Age thresholds (derived)
  ageOver18?: boolean;             // NEW: derived from dateOfBirth
  ageOver21?: boolean;
  
  // Tier 3: Optional
  address?: string;
  gender?: string;
  state?: string;
}

function deriveAgeThresholds(dateOfBirth: string) {
  const dob = new Date(dateOfBirth);
  const today = new Date();
  const age = today.getFullYear() - dob.getFullYear();
  
  // Adjust for birthday not yet occurred this year
  const birthdayThisYear = new Date(today.getFullYear(), dob.getMonth(), dob.getDate());
  const adjustedAge = today >= birthdayThisYear ? age : age - 1;
  
  return {
    ageOver18: adjustedAge >= 18,
    ageOver21: adjustedAge >= 21
  };
}
Frontend: Update Workflow Creation Checkboxes
File: dokimos-app-v2/src/components/WorkflowCreation.tsx (or verifier dashboard)

typescript

Display Format Standards
Nationality — Always Full Country Name:
Bad:

❌ "US"
❌ "USA"
❌ "UK"
Good:

✅ "United States"
✅ "United Kingdom"
✅ "Canada"
✅ "India"
Use ISO 3166 country names (standard country name list).

Date of Birth Format:
Storage (backend):

ISO 8601: "1995-03-15"
Display (frontend for users):

Human-readable: "March 15, 1995"
or
"15 Mar 1995"
Display (frontend for verifiers):

Clear format: "03/15/1995" or "1995-03-15"
Document Type:
Standard values:

"Driver's License"
"Passport"
"National ID Card"
"State ID"
Not: "DL", "license", "passport card" (be specific and consistent)

Use Case Examples
Airbnb Host Verification:
Required Attributes:
☑ Full Name
☑ Age Over 18
☑ Document Not Expired
☐ Address (optional — for local compliance)
Coinbase KYC:
Required Attributes:
☑ Full Name
☑ Date of Birth
☑ Nationality
☑ Address
☑ Document Type
☑ Document Expiry Date
Bar Entry (Age Verification):
Required Attributes:
☑ Age Over 21
☐ Full Name (optional)
Upwork Freelancer:
Required Attributes:
☑ Full Name
☑ Nationality
☑ Age Over 18
Priority for Demo
Must Add:
✅ Age Over 18 (very common threshold)
✅ Nationality with full country names
✅ Date of Birth (more flexible than age thresholds)
Nice to Have:
⚠️ Document Type (adds context)
⚠️ Document Expiry Date (compliance)
Can Skip for Demo:
❌ Address (privacy concerns, complex to display)
❌ Gender (sensitivity, not always needed)
❌ State/Province (less common)

# Task: Add Missing Identity Attributes

## Add the following attributes to the identity verification system:

### Backend (dokimos-tee)

1. **Update ExtractedAttributes interface** to include:
   - `dateOfBirth: string` (ISO format: "1995-03-15")
   - `nationality: string` (full country name: "United States")
   - `documentType: string` ("Driver's License" | "Passport" | "National ID Card")
   - `documentExpiryDate: string` (ISO format: "2028-12-31")
   - `ageOver18: boolean` (derived from dateOfBirth)

2. **Update OCR extraction logic** to parse these fields from ID documents

3. **Add age threshold calculation:**
```typescript
   function calculateAge(dateOfBirth: string): number {
     const dob = new Date(dateOfBirth);
     const today = new Date();
     let age = today.getFullYear() - dob.getFullYear();
     const birthdayThisYear = new Date(today.getFullYear(), dob.getMonth(), dob.getDate());
     if (today < birthdayThisYear) age--;
     return age;
   }
   
   const age = calculateAge(dateOfBirth);
   const ageOver18 = age >= 18;
   const ageOver21 = age >= 21;
```

4. **Nationality formatting:**
   - Always store full country name
   - Map common abbreviations: "US" → "United States", "UK" → "United Kingdom"
   - Use ISO 3166 country names

### Frontend (dokimos-app-v2)

1. **Update available attributes in workflow creation:**
   - Add "Age Over 18" checkbox
   - Add "Date of Birth" checkbox
   - Add "Nationality" checkbox (with note: "Full country name")
   - Add "Document Type" checkbox
   - Add "Document Expiry Date" checkbox

2. **Update vault display to show:**
   - Date of Birth (formatted as "March 15, 1995")
   - Nationality (full country name)
   - Document Type ("Driver's License")
   - Document Expiry Date ("Expires: Dec 31, 2028")

3. **Update attribute display formatting:**
```typescript
   function formatAttribute(key: string, value: any): string {
     switch(key) {
       case 'dateOfBirth':
         return new Date(value).toLocaleDateString('en-US', { 
           year: 'numeric', 
           month: 'long', 
           day: 'numeric' 
         });
       case 'nationality':
         return value; // Already full country name
       case 'documentExpiryDate':
         return `Expires: ${new Date(value).toLocaleDateString()}`;
       default:
         return value;
     }
   }
```

### Testing

After implementation, verify:
- [ ] Age thresholds calculated correctly from DOB
- [ ] Nationality shows full country name (not abbreviation)
- [ ] Document type extracted correctly
- [ ] Dates formatted properly for display
- [ ] Attestation includes new attributes

Must add:

Age Over 18
Nationality (full country names)
Date of Birth
Should add: 4. Document Type 5. Document Expiry Date

Format standards:

Nationality: "United States" (never "US")
Dates: ISO 8601 in backend, human-readable in frontend
Document Type: Full names ("Driver's License" not "DL")

Use the pop-up/notification approach. Don't move this to the top.

Why Pop-up is Better
Current (Empty State Always Visible):
Navigation: [Vault] [Requests] [Settings]
                      ↑
                   You're here

PENDING REQUESTS
┌────────────────────────────────┐
│    🕐                          │

Problems:

❌ Takes up screen space when empty (99% of the time)
❌ User has to navigate to see nothing
❌ Feels like a dead page
❌ Users might forget to check this tab
Better: Notification + Badge
Navigation: [Vault] [Requests ①] [Settings]
                      ↑
                   Badge shows count

Modal:
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐

│  • Document Not Expired             │
│                                     │
│  [Later]  [Review Request →]       │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

Tap "Review Request" → Goes to approval screen
Tap "Later" → Closes modal, badge stays
Advantages:

✅ User sees request immediately (can't miss it)
✅ No empty state taking up space
✅ Badge shows pending count at a glance
✅ More mobile-native UX
✅ Feels responsive (something happened!)

Recommended Flow
Scenario 1: User Opens App, Request Waiting
1. User opens app → Lands on Vault
2. Modal slides up: "Airbnb sent a verification request"
3. User taps "Review" → Goes to approval screen
4. User approves → Request moves to "Where You've Verified"
Scenario 2: User Gets Request While App is Open
1. User browsing app
2. Push notification arrives
3. Modal slides up: "New request from Airbnb"
4. Badge appears on Requests tab (①)
5. User taps "Review" → Approval screen
Scenario 3: User Gets Request, App Closed
1. Push notification: "Airbnb wants to verify you"
2. User taps notification → App opens
3. Modal appears with request details
4. User reviews and approves
Design Recommendation
Don't:
❌ Show big empty state when nothing pending
❌ Make user navigate to empty tab
❌ Leave requests buried in a tab
Do:
✅ Use push notification + modal
✅ Badge on Requests tab when pending
✅ Auto-show modal when request arrives
✅ Keep Requests tab clean (list only, no big empty state)
For Demo
Part 3: Returning User
Don't show:

[Navigate to Requests tab]
[See empty state]
[Wait for request]
Do show:

[User on Vault or home]
[Modal slides up: "Airbnb sent a request"]
[User taps Review]
[Approval screen appears]
Narration:

"Jordan opens Dokimos and sees a notification—Airbnb 
wants to verify her identity.

[Modal appears]

She taps 'Review' to see what they're requesting.

[Approval screen]

Full Name, Age Over 18, Valid ID. She approves.

[Receipt confirmation]

Done. Airbnb has her verified proof instantly."
This feels natural and mobile-native.

TL;DR
Use pop-up/modal + notification, NOT empty state page.

Why:

More mobile-native
Can't miss requests
No wasted screen space
Feels responsive
Better UX
Empty state card should only appear if:

User manually goes to Requests tab
No pending requests
And even then, keep it minimal
Requests should be push-based, not pull-based (user doesn't hunt for them).

markdown
# Task: Implement Request Notification System with Modal Pop-up

Replace the empty state "Pending Requests" page with a notification-driven approach using modals, badges, and a clean requests list.

---

## Phase 1: Remove/Update Empty State

### File: `dokimos-app-v2/src/app/app/requests/page.tsx`

**Current (probably):**
```tsx
// Big empty state card always visible

  
  Nothing pending
  When an organization asks for a verified proof, it will show up here.

```

**Replace with:**
```tsx
'use client';

import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';
import { useSession } from 'next-auth/react';

interface VerificationRequest {
  id: string;
  verifierName: string;
  verifierLogo?: string;
  workflowName: string;
  attributesRequested: string[];
  requestedAt: string;
  status: 'pending' | 'approved' | 'denied';
}

export default function RequestsPage() {
  const { data: session } = useSession();
  const [requests, setRequests] = useState([]);
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);

  useEffect(() => {
    async function fetchRequests() {
      if (!session?.user?.email) return;
      
      try {
        const response = await fetch(`/api/requests/user/${encodeURIComponent(session.user.email)}`);
        const data = await response.json();
        setRequests(data);
      } catch (error) {
        console.error('Failed to fetch requests:', error);
      } finally {
        setLoading(false);
      }
    }
    
    fetchRequests();
  }, [session]);

  const pendingRequests = requests.filter(r => r.status === 'pending');
  const completedRequests = requests.filter(r => r.status !== 'pending');

  if (loading) {
    return Loading requests...;
  }

  return (
    
      Requests
      
      {pendingRequests.length > 0 && (
        
          Pending
          {pendingRequests.map(request => (
            <RequestCard 
              key={request.id} 
              request={request}
              onReview={() => handleReview(request.id)}
            />
          ))}
        
      )}
      
      {completedRequests.length > 0 && (
        
          Completed
          {completedRequests.map(request => (
            
          ))}
        
      )}
      
      {requests.length === 0 && (
        
          No verification requests yet.
        
      )}
    
  );
}

function RequestCard({ request, onReview }: { 
  request: VerificationRequest; 
  onReview: () => void;
}) {
  const timeAgo = getRelativeTime(request.requestedAt);
  
  return (
    
      
        
          {request.verifierName.charAt(0)}
        
        
          {request.verifierName}
          {request.workflowName}
        
        {timeAgo}
      
      
      
        Review →
      
    
  );
}

function CompletedRequestCard({ request }: { request: VerificationRequest }) {
  const timeAgo = getRelativeTime(request.requestedAt);
  const statusIcon = request.status === 'approved' ? '✓' : '✗';
  
  return (
    
      
        
          {request.verifierName.charAt(0)}
        
        
          {request.verifierName}
          
            {statusIcon} {request.status === 'approved' ? 'Approved' : 'Denied'}
          
        
        {timeAgo}
      
    
  );
}

function getRelativeTime(isoString: string): string {
  const now = Date.now();
  const then = new Date(isoString).getTime();
  const diffMs = now - then;
  const diffMins = Math.floor(diffMs / 60000);
  
  if (diffMins < 1) return 'Just now';
  if (diffMins < 60) return `${diffMins} min ago`;
  if (diffMins < 1440) return `${Math.floor(diffMins / 60)} hr ago`;
  return `${Math.floor(diffMins / 1440)} days ago`;
}

function handleReview(requestId: string) {
  // Navigate to approval screen
  window.location.href = `/app/requests/${requestId}/review`;
}
```

## Phase 2: Create Request Notification Modal

### File: Create `dokimos-app-v2/src/components/RequestNotificationModal.tsx`
```tsx
'use client';

import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';
import { X } from 'lucide-react';

interface RequestNotification {
  id: string;
  verifierName: string;
  verifierLogo?: string;
  workflowName: string;
  attributesRequested: string[];
  requestedAt: string;
}

interface RequestNotificationModalProps {
  request: RequestNotification | null;
  onClose: () => void;
  onReview: () => void;
}

export function RequestNotificationModal({ 
  request, 
  onClose, 
  onReview 
}: RequestNotificationModalProps) {
  const [isVisible, setIsVisible] = useState(false);

  useEffect(() => {
    if (request) {
      // Slide up animation
      setTimeout(() => setIsVisible(true), 100);
    } else {
      setIsVisible(false);
    }
  }, [request]);

  if (!request) return null;

  return (
    <>
      {/* Backdrop */}
      
      
      {/* Modal */}
      
        
          Verification Request
          
            
          
        
        
        
          
            
              {request.verifierName.charAt(0)}
            
            {request.verifierName}
          
          
          
            {request.verifierName} wants to verify your identity
            {request.workflowName && ` for ${request.workflowName}`}.
          
          
          
            They're requesting:
            
              {request.attributesRequested.map((attr, index) => (
                {formatAttributeName(attr)}
              ))}
            
          
        
        
        
          
            Later
          
          
            Review Request →
          
        
      
    </>
  );
}

function formatAttributeName(attr: string): string {
  const mapping: Record = {
    'fullName': 'Full Name',
    'ageOver18': 'Age Over 18',
    'ageOver21': 'Age Over 21',
    'documentNotExpired': 'Document Not Expired',
    'nationality': 'Nationality',
    'dateOfBirth': 'Date of Birth',
  };
  
  return mapping[attr] || attr;
}
```

## Phase 3: Add Badge to Requests Tab

### File: Update navigation component (probably `dokimos-app-v2/src/components/Navigation.tsx` or similar)
```tsx
'use client';

import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';
import { useSession } from 'next-auth/react';
import Link from 'next/link';

export function Navigation() {
  const { data: session } = useSession();
  const [pendingCount, setPendingCount] = useState(0);

  useEffect(() => {
    async function fetchPendingCount() {
      if (!session?.user?.email) return;
      
      try {
        const response = await fetch(`/api/requests/user/${encodeURIComponent(session.user.email)}`);
        const requests = await response.json();
        const pending = requests.filter((r: any) => r.status === 'pending');
        setPendingCount(pending.length);
      } catch (error) {
        console.error('Failed to fetch pending count:', error);
      }
    }
    
    fetchPendingCount();
    
    // Poll every 30 seconds for new requests
    const interval = setInterval(fetchPendingCount, 30000);
    return () => clearInterval(interval);
  }, [session]);

  return (
    
      
        Vault
      
      
      
        Requests
        {pendingCount > 0 && (
          {pendingCount}
        )}
      
      
      
        Settings
      
    
  );
}
```

## Phase 4: Add Modal Trigger Logic

### File: Create `dokimos-app-v2/src/hooks/useRequestNotifications.ts`
```typescript
'use client';

import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';
import { useSession } from 'next-auth/react';

interface VerificationRequest {
  id: string;
  verifierName: string;
  verifierLogo?: string;
  workflowName: string;
  attributesRequested: string[];
  requestedAt: string;
  status: 'pending' | 'approved' | 'denied';
}

export function useRequestNotifications() {
  const { data: session } = useSession();
  const [newRequest, setNewRequest] = useState(null);
  const [lastChecked, setLastChecked] = useState(Date.now());

  useEffect(() => {
    if (!session?.user?.email) return;

    async function checkForNewRequests() {
      try {
        const response = await fetch(`/api/requests/user/${encodeURIComponent(session.user.email)}`);
        const requests: VerificationRequest[] = await response.json();
        
        // Find requests created after last check
        const newRequests = requests.filter(r => 
          r.status === 'pending' && 
          new Date(r.requestedAt).getTime() > lastChecked
        );
        
        if (newRequests.length > 0) {
          // Show modal for most recent request
          setNewRequest(newRequests[0]);
          setLastChecked(Date.now());
        }
      } catch (error) {
        console.error('Failed to check for new requests:', error);
      }
    }

    // Check immediately
    checkForNewRequests();

    // Poll every 10 seconds
    const interval = setInterval(checkForNewRequests, 10000);
    
    return () => clearInterval(interval);
  }, [session, lastChecked]);

  const dismissNotification = () => {
    setNewRequest(null);
  };

  return {
    newRequest,
    dismissNotification
  };
}
```

## Phase 5: Integrate Modal in Main Layout

### File: Update `dokimos-app-v2/src/app/app/layout.tsx` or main app component
```tsx
'use client';

import { useRequestNotifications } from '@/hooks/useRequestNotifications';
import { RequestNotificationModal } from '@/components/RequestNotificationModal';
import { useRouter } from 'next/navigation';

export default function AppLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const router = useRouter();
  const { newRequest, dismissNotification } = useRequestNotifications();

  const handleReviewRequest = () => {
    if (newRequest) {
      router.push(`/app/requests/${newRequest.id}/review`);
      dismissNotification();
    }
  };

  return (
    
      {children}
      
      
    
  );
}
```

## Phase 6: API Endpoint for Fetching Requests

### File: Create `dokimos-app-v2/src/app/api/requests/user/[email]/route.ts`
```typescript
import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server';

const TEE_ENDPOINT = process.env.TEE_ENDPOINT || 'http://localhost:8080';

export async function GET(
  req: NextRequest,
  { params }: { params: { email: string } }
) {
  try {
    const email = decodeURIComponent(params.email);
    
    const response = await fetch(`${TEE_ENDPOINT}/api/requests/user/${email}`, {
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }
    });
    
    if (!response.ok) {
      return NextResponse.json([], { status: 200 }); // Return empty array if no requests
    }
    
    const data = await response.json();
    return NextResponse.json(data);
  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Failed to fetch user requests:', error);
    return NextResponse.json([], { status: 200 }); // Return empty array on error
  }
}
```

Layer 2: How It Works (Plain Language)

How Dokimos Verification Works

Think of Dokimos like a notary, but digital and impossible to fake.

1️⃣ Jordan uploaded her ID once
   → Sent to secure, isolated hardware

2️⃣ The hardware checked:
   • Is the ID real and not expired?
   • Does her face match the ID photo?
   • Is she over 18?

3️⃣ The hardware created a digital proof
   → Signed with cryptography (like a tamper-proof seal)

4️⃣ You received that proof
   → You can verify it's real, independent of Dokimos

Why this matters:
- No manual review needed
- Can't be faked or tampered with
- Jordan never uploads her ID again
- Complete audit trail

[← Back]  [Verify It Yourself →]

# Task: Audit Verifier Dashboard Implementation and Prepare for Demo

## Phase 1: Discovery - Find What Exists

First, systematically search the codebase to understand what verifier dashboard components already exist.

### Step 1: Search for Verifier-Related Files

Run these searches and report what you find:
```bash
# Search for "verifier" in file names
find dokimos-app-v2/src -type f -name "*verifier*" -o -name "*Verifier*"

# Search for "verifier" in code
grep -r "verifier" dokimos-app-v2/src --include="*.tsx" --include="*.ts" -l

# Search for "workflow" (workflow creation)
grep -r "workflow" dokimos-app-v2/src --include="*.tsx" --include="*.ts" -l

# Search for "attestation" components
find dokimos-app-v2/src/components -type f -name "*[Aa]ttestation*"

# Search for verification layer/modal
grep -r "VerifyLayer\|VerificationModal\|progressive.*disclosure" dokimos-app-v2/src --include="*.tsx" --include="*.ts" -l

# Check for routes
ls -la dokimos-app-v2/src/app/verifier 2>/dev/null || echo "No /verifier route found"
ls -la dokimos-app-v2/src/app/app/verifier 2>/dev/null || echo "No /app/verifier route found"
ls -la dokimos-app-v2/src/app/dashboard 2>/dev/null || echo "No /dashboard route found"
```

### Step 2: Check for Key Components

Search for specific component patterns:
```bash
# Workflow creation
grep -r "WorkflowForm\|CreateWorkflow\|workflow.*create" dokimos-app-v2/src --include="*.tsx" -l

# Attestations table
grep -r "AttestationsTable\|VerificationTable" dokimos-app-v2/src --include="*.tsx" -l

# Progressive disclosure
grep -r "Layer 1\|Layer 2\|Layer 3\|simple.*verdict\|plain.*language" dokimos-app-v2/src --include="*.tsx" -l

# Attestation detail view
grep -r "AttestationDetail\|VerificationDetail" dokimos-app-v2/src --include="*.tsx" -l
```

### Step 3: Examine Backend Verifier Endpoints

Check if backend has verifier-related endpoints:
```bash
# Check for verifier endpoints in backend
grep -r "verifier\|workflow" dokimos-tee/src --include="*.ts" -l

# Look for specific endpoints
grep -n "POST.*workflow\|GET.*attestations\|verifier.*dashboard" dokimos-tee/src/index.ts
```

## Phase 3: Detailed Component Analysis

For each component that EXISTS, examine its current implementation:

### For Workflow Creation (if found):
```bash
# View the component
cat [path-to-workflow-component]
```

**Answer these questions:**
1. Does it have a text input for workflow name?
2. Does it have checkboxes for attributes (fullName, ageOver18, etc.)?
3. Does it have a purpose/description field?
4. Does it actually call a backend API or is it static?
5. Does it match this design spec:
Workflow Name: [] Required Attributes: ☑ Full Name ☑ Age Over 18 ☐ Age Over 21 ☑ Document Not Expired Purpose: [] [Create Workflow]


### For Attestations Table (if found):
```bash
# View the component
cat [path-to-attestations-table]
```

**Answer these questions:**
1. Does it show columns: Name, Workflow, Status, Verified?
2. Is it a table or cards?
3. Does it have pagination?
4. Does it have search/filter?
5. Is data hardcoded or from API?
6. Can you click rows to see details?
7. Does it match this design:
Name Workflow Status Verified ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── Jordan Lee Host Verification ✓ Verified Today 2:34 PM


### For Progressive Disclosure (if found):
```bash
# View the component  
cat [path-to-verify-layer-or-modal]
```

**Answer these questions:**
1. Does it have 3 distinct layers?
2. Layer 1: Simple verdict with checkmarks?
3. Layer 2: Plain language with numbered steps?
4. Layer 3: Technical with expandable sections?
5. Are there transitions between layers?
6. Does it match the spec in our demo design?

## Phase 4: Gap Analysis

Based on what you found, identify what needs to be built/fixed:

### Critical Gaps (Must Fix for Demo):

**If Workflow Creation is MISSING:**

Form with text input for workflow name
Checkbox list for attributes
Purpose textarea
Create button

**If Attestations Table is MISSING:**
Need to build:

Table component with sortable columns
Row click handler to open detail view
Status badges (✓ Verified, ⏳ Pending)
Seed data with Jordan Lee + 4-5 others
Responsive layout

**If Attestation Detail is MISSING:**
Need to build:

Detail view showing:
User name and workflow
Status and timestamp
Verified attributes list
Attestation metadata (signature, signer, hash)
TEE information (platform, enclave, debug mode)
[Verify Attestation] button

**If Progressive Disclosure is MISSING or INCOMPLETE:**
Need to build:

Layer 1: Simple verdict screen
Layer 2: How it works (plain language)
Layer 3: Technical step-by-step with:
5 verification steps
Progress bar
Expandable "What this proves" sections
Links to EigenCloud/GitHub

### Medium Priority Gaps:

- Search/filter functionality
- Export to CSV
- Real backend integration
- Authentication

### Low Priority (Can Skip for Demo):

- Real-time updates
- Advanced filtering
- Dashboard analytics
- Multi-user support

This is the design language for the verifier side, which looks completely unprofessional and different from the design language for the app side. That is so much more well thought out and so much cleaner to see and easier to understand. Can you give me a prompt to fix that and make this feel much more cohesive between both the app and this, because it looks really unprofessional right now?

1:07 PM
markdown
# Task: Redesign Verifier Dashboard to Match User App Design Language

## Problem

The verifier dashboard has a completely different design language from the user app:
- **Verifier side:** Dark blue background, purple button, cluttered text, outdated feel
- **User app:** Clean white backgrounds, black buttons, minimal design, modern typography

This inconsistency looks unprofessional and breaks the product experience.

## Goal

Redesign the verifier dashboard to feel cohesive with the user app while maintaining appropriate distinction between consumer and business interfaces.

---

## Phase 1: Analyze Current User App Design System

### Extract Design Tokens from User App

Look at `dokimos-app-v2/src/` and identify:

**Colors:**
```typescript
// Primary colors used in user app
const colors = {
  background: '#FFFFFF',           // White backgrounds
  surface: '#F9FAFB',             // Light gray surfaces
  primary: '#1a1a1a',             // Black for primary actions
  text: {
    primary: '#1a1a1a',           // Dark text
    secondary: '#666666',         // Gray text
    tertiary: '#999999'           // Light gray text
  },
  border: '#E5E7EB',              // Subtle borders
  success: '#059669',             // Green for verified
  error: '#EF4444'                // Red for errors
}
```

**Typography:**
```typescript
// Font families used
const fonts = {
  heading: 'Instrument Serif',    // Emotional headlines
  body: 'Geist',                  // Everything else
  mono: 'Geist Mono'              // Code/technical
}
```

**Component Patterns:**
```typescript
// Button styles
- Primary: Black background, white text, 12px border-radius
- Secondary: Light gray background, dark text
- Minimal padding, clean corners

// Cards
- White background
- Subtle border or shadow
- 12-16px border-radius
- 16-24px padding

// Form inputs
- White background
- Light border (#E5E7EB)
- 8-12px border-radius
- Clear labels above inputs
```

### New Design (Clean):

**File:** `dokimos-verifier/src/app/signin/page.tsx` (or wherever this lives)
```tsx
export default function VerifierSignIn() {
  return (
    <div className="signin-page">
      {/* Minimal header */}
      <header className="header">
        <div className="logo">
          <BuildingIcon size={24} />
          <span>Dokimos for Business</span>
        </div>
      </header>

      {/* Main content */}
      <main className="content">
        <div className="signin-card">
          <h1>Sign in</h1>
          <p className="subtitle">
            Access your verification dashboard
          </p>

          <form className="signin-form">
            <div className="form-field">
              <label htmlFor="email">Email</label>
              <input
                id="email"
                type="email"
                placeholder="your@company.com"
              />
            </div>

            <div className="form-field">
              <label htmlFor="password">Password</label>
              <input
                id="password"
                type="password"
                placeholder="••••••••"
              />
            </div>

            <button type="submit" className="signin-button">
              Sign in
            </button>
          </form>

          <div className="footer-link">
            Don't have an account? <a href="/signup">Contact sales</a>
          </div>
        </div>

        {/* Demo credentials - subtle */}
        <div className="demo-hint">
          Demo: airbnb@business.com / demo
        </div>
      </main>
    </div>
  );
}
```

**Styles:**

## Phase 3: Create Unified Design System

### File: Create `dokimos-verifier/src/styles/design-system.css`
```css
/* 
  Dokimos Design System
  Shared between user app and verifier dashboard
*/

:root {
  /* Colors */
  --color-background: #FFFFFF;
  --color-surface: #F9FAFB;
  --color-primary: #1a1a1a;
  --color-text-primary: #1a1a1a;
  --color-text-secondary: #666666;
  --color-text-tertiary: #999999;
  --color-border: #E5E7EB;
  --color-success: #059669;
  --color-error: #EF4444;
  --color-warning: #F59E0B;
  
  /* Typography */
  --font-heading: 'Instrument Serif', serif;
  --font-body: 'Geist', sans-serif;
  --font-mono: 'Geist Mono', monospace;
  
  /* Spacing */
  --spacing-xs: 4px;
  --spacing-sm: 8px;
  --spacing-md: 12px;
  --spacing-lg: 16px;
  --spacing-xl: 24px;
  --spacing-2xl: 32px;
  
  /* Border radius */
  --radius-sm: 8px;
  --radius-md: 12px;
  --radius-lg: 16px;
  
  /* Shadows */
  --shadow-sm: 0 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.05);
  --shadow-md: 0 4px 6px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.07);
  --shadow-lg: 0 10px 15px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
}

/* Typography styles */
.heading-xl {
  font-family: var(--font-heading);
  font-size: 2rem;
  font-weight: 400;
  color: var(--color-text-primary);
}

.heading-lg {
  font-family: var(--font-heading);
  font-size: 1.5rem;
  font-weight: 400;
  color: var(--color-text-primary);
}

.body {
  font-family: var(--font-body);
  font-size: 0.9375rem;
  color: var(--color-text-primary);
}

.body-secondary {
  font-family: var(--font-body);
  font-size: 0.875rem;
  color: var(--color-text-secondary);
}

/* Button styles */
.button-primary {
  padding: 14px 20px;
  background: var(--color-primary);
  color: white;
  border: none;
  border-radius: var(--radius-sm);
  font-family: var(--font-body);
  font-size: 0.9375rem;
  font-weight: 500;
  cursor: pointer;
  transition: background 0.2s;
}

.button-primary:hover {
  background: #2a2a2a;
}

.button-secondary {
  padding: 14px 20px;
  background: var(--color-surface);
  color: var(--color-text-primary);
  border: 1px solid var(--color-border);
  border-radius: var(--radius-sm);
  font-family: var(--font-body);
  font-size: 0.9375rem;
  font-weight: 500;
  cursor: pointer;
  transition: background 0.2s;
}

.button-secondary:hover {
  background: #F3F4F6;
}

/* Card styles */
.card {
  background: var(--color-background);
  border: 1px solid var(--color-border);
  border-radius: var(--radius-md);
  padding: var(--spacing-xl);
}

/* Input styles */
.input {
  padding: 12px 16px;
  border: 1px solid var(--color-border);
  border-radius: var(--radius-sm);
  font-family: var(--font-body);
  font-size: 0.9375rem;
  background: var(--color-background);
  transition: border-color 0.2s;
}

.input:focus {
  outline: none;
  border-color: var(--color-primary);
}
```

## Phase 6: Distinguish Business vs Consumer (Subtle)

While using the same design system, add subtle distinctions:

### User App (Consumer):
- Warmer, friendlier
- More hand-holding copy
- Simpler language

### Verifier Dashboard (Business):
- More professional tone
- Dense information display (tables, data)
- Business terminology acceptable

**But SAME:**
- Colors
- Typography
- Component styles
- Border radius
- Spacing

Go through EVERY verifier page and:

- [ ] Remove all dark blue backgrounds → Replace with white
- [ ] Remove all purple buttons → Replace with black
- [ ] Remove all centered card layouts → Use full-width with proper padding
- [ ] Replace all custom colors → Use design system variables
- [ ] Replace all font families → Use Instrument Serif + Geist
- [ ] Replace all button styles → Use .button-primary / .button-secondary
- [ ] Replace all form inputs → Use .input class
- [ ] Replace all cards → Use .card class
- [ ] Add proper spacing → Use design system spacing variables
- [ ] Remove gradients → Use flat colors only
- [ ] Fix "Dokimos for Business" header → Make it clean and minimal

## Quick Wins (Do These First)

1. **Replace all backgrounds:**
```css
   /* Find and replace */
   background: #1e3a5f → background: #FFFFFF
   background: #2c5282 → background: #FFFFFF
```

2. **Replace all buttons:**
```css
   /* Find and replace */
   background: #6366F1 → background: #1a1a1a
   background: #4F46E5 → background: #1a1a1a
```

3. **Import design system:**
```tsx
   import '@/styles/design-system.css'
```

4. **Use design tokens:**
```css
   color: #1a1a1a → color: var(--color-text-primary)
   border: 1px solid #E5E7EB → border: 1px solid var(--color-border)
```

Better Approach: Cohesive But Distinct
The ChatGPT vs OpenAI Example (Perfect Reference)
What They Share (Brand Cohesion):
✅ Same typeface (likely a custom sans-serif)
✅ Same corner radius on buttons/cards
✅ Same general spacing system
✅ Recognizable as "the same company"
What's Different (Audience Distinction):
ChatGPT (Consumer):

Softer colors (warm grays, friendly greens)
Rounded, friendly UI
Conversational copy
Simple, clean layouts
Emoji and illustrations
More white space
OpenAI Platform (Developer):

Technical colors (cool grays, code editor vibes)
Denser information layouts
Technical terminology
Monospace fonts for code
Tables, API docs, technical data
More information per screen
Key insight: They feel related but serve different needs.

How to Apply This to Dokimos
Shared Elements (Brand DNA):
Typography family — Instrument Serif + Geist
Border radius — 8-12px consistently
Spacing scale — Same spacing system
Core black — 
#1a1a1a for primary elements
Logo/wordmark — Same across both
Different Elements (Audience Signals):
User App (Consumer - Friendly):

Warmer grays (
#F9FAFB backgrounds)
Larger text, more breathing room
One thing per screen
Emotional headlines ("Verified ✓", "Never upload again")
Friendly copy
Illustrations/icons with personality
Cards with soft shadows
More vertical spacing
Verifier Dashboard (Business - Professional):

Cooler grays (
#F5F5F5 backgrounds)
Denser layouts (tables, multiple columns)

Business-focused headlines ("Host Verifications", "Attestation Details")
Professional copy
Data-first design (tables, metrics, charts)

Revised Design Strategy
1. Color System (Subtle Shift)
User App (Warm):

css
:root {
  /* Backgrounds */
  --bg-primary: #FFFFFF;
  --bg-secondary: #F9FAFB;      /* Warmer gray */
  --bg-tertiary: #F3F4F6;
  
  /* Text */
  --text-primary: #1a1a1a;
  --text-secondary: #666666;
  --text-tertiary: #999999;
  
  /* Accents */
  --accent-success: #059669;     /* Warmer green */
  --accent-primary: #1a1a1a;
  
  /* Borders */
  --border-subtle: #E5E7EB;
  --border-strong: #D1D5DB;
}
Verifier Dashboard (Cool/Technical):

css
:root {
  /* Backgrounds */
  --bg-primary: #FFFFFF;
  --bg-secondary: #F5F5F5;      /* Cooler gray */
  --bg-tertiary: #EEEEEE;
  --bg-code: #F8F9FA;           /* Code-like backgrounds */
  
  /* Text */
  --text-primary: #1a1a1a;
  --text-secondary: #666666;
  --text-tertiary: #999999;
  
  /* Accents */
  --accent-success: #10B981;     /* Cooler green */
  --accent-primary: #1a1a1a;
  --accent-technical: #6366F1;   /* Technical blue for data/status */
  
  /* Borders */
  --border-subtle: #E5E7EB;
  --border-strong: #D1D5DB;
  --border-table: #E0E0E0;       /* Table-specific */
}
Key difference: Warmer vs cooler undertones, but same core structure.

css

}

}

css

  font-family: 'Instrument Serif', serif;

  font-weight: 400;
  line-height: 1.1;
  margin-bottom: 8px;            /* Tighter */
}

p {
  font-size: 0.875rem;           /* More compact */
  line-height: 1.5;              /* Efficient */
}

/* Add monospace for technical data */
.technical-data {
  font-family: 'Geist Mono', monospace;
  font-size: 0.8125rem;
}
